q: Your organization has GuardDuty enabled in multiple AWS regions. A finding shows UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/SSHBruteForce for an instance. What should you do to investigate and remediate?
options: Immediately terminate the instance without investigation
Review CloudTrail logs for the instance, check security group rules, and isolate the instance if compromised
Change the instance's VPC security group to allow all outbound traffic
Disable GuardDuty for the region to prevent false positives
explain: When GuardDuty generates an SSH brute force finding, the correct response is to review CloudTrail logs for any successful authentication attempts following the failures, inspect the instance's security group to confirm port 22 exposure, and isolate the instance if evidence of compromise is found. Immediate termination without investigation destroys forensic evidence, while disabling GuardDuty removes threat visibility. Changing security groups to allow all outbound traffic would worsen the security posture.

q: GuardDuty detected CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B!DNS activity on an EC2 instance. Which AWS service provides detailed investigation capabilities?
options: Security Hub to dismiss findings
AWS Detective to correlate events and investigate
CloudTrail alone to see API calls
VPC Flow Logs alone to see network traffic
explain: Amazon Detective is designed specifically to accelerate security investigations by correlating data from GuardDuty findings, CloudTrail API calls, VPC Flow Logs, and CloudWatch Events into interactive entity-behavior graphs. For a CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B!DNS finding, Detective can show the full timeline of the instance's network connections and related IAM activity. Dismissing findings in Security Hub or reviewing a single log source in isolation would provide incomplete investigation context.

q: GuardDuty finds UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/MaliciousIPCaller.Custom indicating an EC2 instance is making requests to a known malicious IP. This requires automatic remediation. How should you configure this?
options: Manually review each finding weekly
Enable GuardDuty's built-in auto-isolation feature
Use EventBridge to route GuardDuty findings to Lambda, which isolates the instance's security group and notifies security team
Block the IP in WAF rules
explain: Amazon EventBridge can detect GuardDuty findings in near real-time and route them to a Lambda function that calls the EC2 API to apply a restrictive security group, effectively isolating the instance while notifying the security team via SNS. GuardDuty has no built-in auto-isolation capability; it is a detection-only service. AWS WAF operates at the HTTP layer for inbound traffic and cannot block outbound EC2 calls to malicious IPs.

q: An EC2 instance shows GuardDuty finding Trojan:EC2/PhishingEmail but your team received no external threat alerts. How do you validate if this is a legitimate threat or false positive?
options: Query CloudTrail for PutObject calls to S3 or SendEmail calls via SES to detect actual phishing transmission
Delete the instance immediately
Assume it's a false positive because there are no external alerts
Ignore the finding
explain: Validating a Trojan:EC2/PhishingEmail finding requires cross-referencing GuardDuty behavioral detection with CloudTrail evidence of actual suspicious API calls such as PutObject to S3 or SendEmail via SES, which would confirm actual phishing activity. Without corroborating evidence in CloudTrail, the finding may represent heuristic detection of a benign process. Deleting the instance immediately or ignoring the finding are both inappropriate without first determining whether a real threat exists.

q: Your organization must integrate GuardDuty findings with a third-party SIEM. What is the most efficient approach?
options: Use EventBridge to route findings to SNS or HTTP endpoints that your SIEM subscribes to
Manually log into GuardDuty console and copy findings
Export findings to S3 daily and have SIEM poll the bucket
Use GuardDuty's built-in SIEM integration feature
explain: Amazon EventBridge is the AWS-native service for routing GuardDuty findings in real-time to downstream targets, including SNS topics or HTTPS endpoints that a SIEM can subscribe to. Manual console review and daily S3 exports introduce unacceptable latency for security event response. GuardDuty does not have a built-in SIEM integration feature; EventBridge is always required as the integration layer.

q: GuardDuty detected UnauthorizedAccess:RDS/AuthorizationFailure multiple times from a developer account. What should you investigate?
options: Enable RDS encryption (if not already enabled)
Block the developer's IP address
Immediately revoke the developer's access
Check CloudTrail for failed RDS authentication attempts, review RDS security group rules, and verify IAM database authentication tokens if enabled
explain: When GuardDuty detects repeated RDS authentication failures, the correct approach is to review CloudTrail for failed RDS API calls, inspect the RDS security group for unexpected inbound rules, and verify whether IAM database authentication tokens are properly scoped. Simply blocking the IP or immediately revoking access may disrupt legitimate users or miss the root cause. Enabling encryption addresses a different concern and does not help diagnose repeated authentication failures.

q: You're running a Security Hub standard and GuardDuty integration. How many regions should GuardDuty cover for a multi-region AWS deployment?
options: Only the primary region to reduce costs
Only regions with production workloads
All regions where you have resources to detect threats everywhere
No regions; use Security Hub findings instead
explain: GuardDuty must be enabled in every AWS region where resources exist because threats can originate from or target any region, and GuardDuty only analyzes data within the region where it is enabled. Restricting GuardDuty to only the primary or production region creates blind spots that attackers can exploit. Security Hub aggregates findings but cannot substitute for GuardDuty's actual threat detection in each region.

q: A GuardDuty finding indicates CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B!DNS on an instance that is part of an Auto Scaling group. What's the best remediation approach?
options: Disable ASG temporarily
Just update the instance's IAM role
Isolate the instance security group immediately, preserve for forensics, create a new AMI from a clean backup, and update ASG launch template to prevent re-infection
Terminate the instance and let ASG launch a replacement
explain: For a confirmed CryptoCurrency finding on an Auto Scaling group member, the proper response is to immediately isolate the instance's security group to stop lateral movement, preserve the instance for forensic analysis, create a clean AMI from a known-good backup, and update the ASG launch template to prevent re-infection. Simply terminating the instance destroys forensic evidence and does not address the root cause if the AMI is compromised. Updating only the IAM role does not stop the active mining activity.

q: Your organization receives high-volume GuardDuty findings. Some are business-as-usual activity. How do you reduce false positives?
options: Disable GuardDuty
Ignore all findings
Create trusted IP lists and whitelisted accounts in GuardDuty settings to refine threat detection accuracy
Use only CloudTrail instead
explain: GuardDuty supports trusted IP lists and threat intelligence whitelists that allow security teams to suppress findings from known-good IP addresses such as corporate NAT gateways or authorized security scanners, reducing false positive volume while maintaining detection for unknown sources. Disabling GuardDuty entirely removes all threat visibility, which is not an acceptable response to alert fatigue. Switching to CloudTrail alone would provide only API-level activity data without GuardDuty's machine learning-based behavioral detection.

q: You need to automatically revoke credentials of a compromised IAM user detected by GuardDuty. Which service orchestrates this?
options: CloudTrail prevents the compromise
EventBridge triggers Lambda, which calls IAM API to delete access keys and revoke sessions
Manually review and revoke credentials
GuardDuty automatically revokes credentials
explain: Amazon EventBridge detects GuardDuty findings and routes them to a Lambda function, which can call the IAM API to deactivate the compromised user's access keys (iam:UpdateAccessKey) and revoke active sessions by attaching an inline deny policy. GuardDuty is a detection-only service and does not perform any automatic credential remediation. CloudTrail captures what happened but cannot block ongoing access; only IAM API calls can revoke the credentials.

q: A GuardDuty finding shows PenTest:IAMUser/KaliLinux accessed your AWS account. What should you verify?
options: Check if this aligns with scheduled penetration testing, review CloudTrail for what was accessed, and verify testing authorization
Assume it's a real attack
Ignore it as GuardDuty is known to flag pen test tools
Immediately revoke all credentials
explain: A PenTest:IAMUser/KaliLinux finding requires verification that the activity aligns with a scheduled and authorized penetration test before taking remediation action. CloudTrail provides the scope of what was accessed, enabling the security team to confirm whether the activity matched the authorized test plan. Immediately revoking credentials or ignoring the finding without evidence gathering could either disrupt legitimate testing or allow an actual attack to continue.

q: Detective is enabled in your account. An EC2 instance shows suspicious outbound connections. How does Detective help investigate?
options: Detective sends an alert email
Detective blocks the outbound connections
Detective builds an entity graph showing the instance's behaviors, related resources, and timeline to correlate with GuardDuty findings and CloudTrail events
Detective automatically terminates the instance
explain: Amazon Detective builds entity-behavior graphs by continuously ingesting data from GuardDuty, CloudTrail, and VPC Flow Logs, enabling investigators to visualize an EC2 instance's outbound connection patterns, correlated API activity, and deviation from historical baselines. Unlike raw log analysis, Detective provides pre-computed relationships and timelines that dramatically reduce investigation time. Detective does not send alerts or block connections; it is a pure investigation tool.

q: You're investigating a potential breach. Detective shows an IAM user's access key was created on day 1, then used from multiple suspicious IPs on day 2. What should you do?
options: Wait for Security Hub to alert
Nothing, Detective only shows findings
Mark it as resolved in Detective
Review CloudTrail for what API calls the key made, isolate the user account, revoke the access key, and change the password
explain: When Detective reveals an access key was created and then used from multiple countries within minutes, the appropriate response is to immediately delete the access key, review CloudTrail for all API actions taken with it, revoke any active sessions by attaching a deny policy, and rotate any secrets that may have been accessed. Geographic spread of credential use in seconds is a strong indicator of compromise and warrants immediate containment rather than waiting for more evidence. Creating a new access key without addressing the compromise does nothing to stop the attacker.

q: Macie identified a bucket containing 50,000 unencrypted files with PII. What should you do?
options: Ignore the finding
Review findings, encrypt objects with KMS, create S3 Object Lock with legal hold, enable versioning, and block public access
Move files to an encrypted bucket manually for each file
Delete the bucket
explain: Amazon Macie's finding of 50,000 unencrypted PII files requires both immediate remediation and protective measures: encrypting objects with a KMS CMK prevents future unauthorized access, S3 Object Lock with legal hold prevents deletion of evidence, and versioning enables recovery of prior states. Reviewing the findings first is critical to understand the scope before applying controls. Manually moving or deleting files risks data loss and does not address the underlying access control problem.

q: Macie is finding sensitive data stored in S3 buckets across your account. How should you remediate at scale?
options: Manually review each bucket
Create an EventBridge rule that triggers Lambda to apply bucket encryption, enable Object Lock, and apply bucket policies to block unencrypted uploads
Disable Amazon Macie to prevent it from generating findings that require investigation
Use Macie's built-in remediation to automatically encrypt all findings
explain: Macie identifies sensitive data but does not perform remediation; automated remediation at scale requires an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function to apply S3 bucket encryption, enable Object Lock, and add bucket policies denying unencrypted uploads whenever Macie generates a finding. Macie has no built-in remediation capability. Disabling Macie removes sensitive data visibility, which is the opposite of what a security-conscious organization should do.

q: Inspector found a critical CVE on an EC2 instance running an outdated package version. What is the immediate remediation step?
options: Terminate the instance immediately
Disable Inspector
Update the package to a patched version, then verify the instance works, and update the AMI to prevent future instances from being vulnerable
Apply WAF rules to block requests to the vulnerable service
explain: When Amazon Inspector finds a critical CVE in an application package, the correct remediation is to update the vulnerable package to its patched version, validate the fix does not break application functionality, and then bake a new AMI to prevent future instances from inheriting the vulnerability. Applying only WAF rules addresses the network perimeter but not the root cause vulnerability in the software stack. Disabling Inspector removes the ongoing vulnerability monitoring that prevents future exposures.

q: You enabled Inspector for EC2 scanning. The scan found a critical finding. How long does Inspector require to resolve findings?
options: Inspector automatically resolves findings after 24 hours
Inspector continuously monitors and resolves findings automatically
Inspector only identifies findings; you must remediate and request re-scan in Inspector to confirm resolution
You manually mark findings as resolved in Inspector
explain: Amazon Inspector is a vulnerability scanner, not a remediation engine; it identifies findings but requires you to patch the software and then trigger a re-scan to confirm the vulnerability is resolved. Inspector does not have an automatic resolution timer or self-healing capability. Marking findings as resolved in Inspector without actual patching would falsely indicate compliance.

q: Inspector scans your Lambda functions and finds vulnerable dependencies. How should you remediate?
options: Use Lambda@Edge to filter requests
Disable the Lambda function
Lambda functions can't have vulnerable dependencies
Update function code to remove vulnerable dependencies, redeploy, and trigger Inspector re-scan to confirm
explain: Lambda functions package application dependencies (Python packages via pip, Node.js modules via npm) in deployment packages or layers, and these dependencies can contain CVEs just like OS-level packages. Remediation requires updating the vulnerable library in the source code, redeploying the function, and triggering an Inspector re-scan to verify resolution. Lambda@Edge filtering addresses inbound traffic, not vulnerable code execution within the function itself.

q: Your organization has GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, and Detective all enabled. What should be the incident response priority order?
options: Handle all findings equally
React to GuardDuty findings first, then Macie, then Inspector
Investigate with Detective first to understand context, prioritize based on severity and business impact, then remediate
Ignore Inspector since it's for vulnerabilities, not threats
explain: In a multi-service security environment with GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, and Detective, Amazon Detective should be the starting point for investigations because it correlates findings across all sources and provides entity-relationship context that helps determine if separate findings are part of a coordinated attack. After establishing investigation context, prioritization should be based on business impact and severity rather than source service. Inspector findings (vulnerabilities) and Macie findings (data exposure) require different response tracks from active threats in GuardDuty.

q: A GuardDuty finding shows an EC2 instance accessing Kubernetes cluster metadata. What does this indicate?
options: Normal activity
Kubernetes clusters can't be compromised on AWS
Potential container escape or misconfiguration; investigate with Detective, check CloudTrail for EKS API calls, review pod security policies
The instance should be terminated
explain: An EC2 instance accessing Kubernetes metadata unexpectedly suggests either a legitimate EKS node communication (which should be verified), a container escape exploit, or a misconfigured IAM role granting excessive metadata access. The correct response is to investigate with Detective, review CloudTrail for EKS API calls (DescribeCluster, ListPods), and audit pod security policies to understand the blast radius. The Kubernetes API surface is a high-value target, making prompt investigation critical.

q: You're designing a threat detection pipeline. Where should GuardDuty findings flow for automated response?
options: Only to CloudTrail
EventBridge -> Lambda -> SNS (for critical findings) + CloudWatch Logs (audit trail)
EventBridge -> SNS -> manual review
To email directly
explain: The best-practice GuardDuty remediation pipeline is: EventBridge captures findings in real time, Lambda applies automated remediation logic and filters by severity, SNS notifies the security team for critical findings, and CloudWatch Logs maintains an immutable audit trail of all remediation actions taken. Sending findings only to CloudTrail loses the automation capability. Email-only notifications provide no automation and no audit trail.

q: GuardDuty's PenTest:IAMUser/KaliLinux finding requires verification that it's authorized testing. What evidence should you gather?
options: Immediately revoke credentials
Terminate the user account
Check if testing was scheduled with security team, review CloudTrail for scope of access (which services/regions), compare against authorization document
Assume it's a false alarm
explain: For authorized penetration tests, the evidence gathering process requires confirming authorization documentation exists with the security team, reviewing CloudTrail to determine which services and regions were accessed, and comparing the actual access scope against the approved test plan. Immediately revoking credentials before verification could disrupt an authorized engagement. Assuming the finding is a false alarm without evidence risks ignoring an actual attack.

q: Detective shows an EC2 instance was created, then immediately assumed an IAM role with admin permissions, then created an S3 bucket. This correlation suggests an automated attack. How do you respond?
options: Assume it's normal activity
Wait to investigate
Terminate the EC2 instance immediately, revoke the IAM role, delete the S3 bucket, query CloudTrail for all actions, check for lateral movement
Only delete the bucket
explain: When Detective reveals an automated attack sequence (EC2 instance → admin role assumption → S3 bucket creation), the response must be comprehensive: terminate the EC2 instance to stop the attack, revoke the IAM role to prevent further escalation, delete the attacker-created S3 bucket to remove their foothold, query CloudTrail for all API actions taken, and check for lateral movement to other accounts or regions. Acting on only part of the kill chain (like deleting just the bucket) allows the attacker to continue using other compromised resources.

q: Security Hub shows 500 findings from Inspector (CVEs) and 50 from GuardDuty (threats). How do you prioritize response?
options: Respond to findings randomly
Ignore Inspector findings
Fix all CVEs first
Filter by severity: GuardDuty active threats are highest priority, Inspector CVEs by severity and exploitability, use Security Hub insights to group related findings
explain: Security Hub's findings should be prioritized using a risk-based approach: GuardDuty active threat findings represent in-progress attacks and are the highest priority regardless of severity score, followed by Inspector CVE findings sorted by CVSS score and exploitability using EPSS data. Security Hub custom insights allow grouping related findings to identify coordinated attack patterns. Treating all findings equally or ignoring entire source services creates dangerous security gaps.

q: GuardDuty is disabled in your production region to reduce costs. An attacker exploits this blind spot. What's the lesson?
options: Production doesn't need threat detection
Threats can't occur in production
GuardDuty should be enabled in all regions, especially production, regardless of cost; threat detection is not optional
Costs must be minimized
explain: Disabling GuardDuty in production environments to reduce costs is a critical security mistake because threat detection is a baseline security control, not an optional cost center. Without GuardDuty, active threats including credential compromise, data exfiltration, and lateral movement go undetected until significant damage has occurred. The cost of GuardDuty is typically orders of magnitude less than the cost of a security breach.

q: An Inspector scan found a low-severity finding (informational). Should you ignore it?
options: All low-severity findings can be ignored
All findings must be fixed immediately
Severity alone determines response
Context matters: if the finding affects a sensitive workload or has exploitability, investigate; if it's on a non-critical service, you can defer but track it
explain: Inspector findings should be prioritized by risk context, not severity score alone: a low-severity finding on a publicly accessible service processing sensitive data may warrant faster remediation than a medium finding on an isolated internal tool. Tracking all findings, even deferred ones, ensures they are not forgotten during the next patching cycle. Ignoring low-severity findings entirely misses the reality that many real-world exploits chain multiple low-severity issues together.

q: GuardDuty finding: CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B on instance. First step is isolation. How do you preserve evidence?
options: Detach network interface from instance, keep EBS volumes attached for analysis, create AMI for forensics, preserve CloudTrail logs
Just reboot the instance
Ignore the finding
Immediately terminate instance
explain: When isolating a potentially compromised EC2 instance, the correct forensic preservation approach is to detach the network interface (stopping the attack while keeping the instance running), preserve all EBS volumes with their data intact for disk forensics, create an AMI for later analysis, and ensure CloudTrail logs from the incident period are secured. Immediate termination destroys all volatile state and can leave attached EBS volumes in an inconsistent state. Simply rebooting the instance erases memory artifacts without stopping the attack.

q: Inspector detects Severity=MEDIUM CVE in production Lambda running once per day. Should you patch immediately?
options: Yes; deploy patch to new version before next execution
MEDIUM vulnerabilities don't matter
No, MEDIUM is not critical
Defer investigation until the weekly security review meeting scheduled for next Friday
explain: A medium-severity Inspector CVE in a production Lambda function should be patched before the next execution by deploying a new function version with the updated dependency, because even medium-severity vulnerabilities can be actively exploited and Lambda's execution model means each invocation processes real workload. The patching window for a once-daily function is measured in hours, not weeks. Deferring medium findings without time-bounding creates unmanaged vulnerability debt.

q: GuardDuty finding CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B is confirmed real. Attacker active 2 days. What's the incident response priority?
options: Ignore since 2 days passed
Only isolate the instance
Immediate: isolate instance, revoke IAM credentials, check CloudTrail for lateral movement, review S3/RDS for data exfiltration
Create ticket and investigate tomorrow
explain: A confirmed 2-day active cryptomining compromise requires immediate parallel actions: isolate the EC2 instance (security group revocation), revoke all IAM credentials the instance could have accessed (including instance role credentials), query CloudTrail for lateral movement to other services, and review S3 and RDS access logs for data exfiltration. A 2-day window provides significant time for an attacker to establish persistence mechanisms, exfiltrate data, or compromise additional resources. Deferring investigation compounds the damage.

q: Detective reveals an EC2 instance accessed via SSH at 3 AM from an unusual country. Is this definitely an attack?
options: Context needed: Check if team member traveled, if SSH key is compromised, review commands executed via CloudTrail/system logs
Yes, it's definitely an attack
Ignore geographic anomalies
No, unusual country means nothing
explain: Geographic anomalies in SSH access require context before concluding compromise: a team member traveling internationally, VPN usage from another country, or automation using a cloud-based system can all explain unusual source geography. CloudTrail shows what commands or API calls were executed during the session, which is a stronger indicator of malicious intent than geography alone. Treating geographic anomalies as definitive indicators of attack leads to unnecessary disruption of legitimate access.

q: Macie found 1,000 files with credit card data (PCI) in unencrypted S3 bucket. This violates PCI-DSS. What's the immediate remediation?
options: Move to on-premises
File a breach report first
Delete the files
Encrypt bucket with KMS, enable Object Lock for PCI compliance, review access logs, notify security team and legal
explain: When Macie confirms unencrypted PCI data in an S3 bucket, the immediate remediation sequence is: encrypt the bucket with KMS (prevent future unencrypted storage), enable Object Lock (prevent evidence deletion), review S3 server access logs and CloudTrail data events (determine if unauthorized access occurred), and notify the security and legal teams (enable breach assessment). Filing a breach report before understanding the scope and taking remediation steps is premature and may trigger unnecessary regulatory actions.

q: GuardDuty detects a finding that looks like false positive. You whitelist the IP. Finding stops. But how do you know if it's truly benign?
options: CloudTrail review: check what actual actions were taken from that IP
Assume all false positives are benign
Never whitelist
Trust the whitelist
explain: Whitelisting a GuardDuty IP suppresses future findings but does not retroactively validate past behavior; the only way to confirm benign activity from that IP is to query CloudTrail for the actual API calls made from that address during the finding window and verify they align with expected activity. Assumptions about whitelisted IPs being safe create blind spots if an IP address is later reused by an attacker or shared via NAT. All whitelisting decisions should be documented with supporting CloudTrail evidence.

q: Detective shows an IAM access key was created, then 10 minutes later used from 5 different countries. What should you do?
options: Assume it's a false alarm
Immediately delete the access key, query CloudTrail for all actions, revoke sessions, rotate secrets
Wait for more evidence
Create a new access key
explain: Access key use from 5 geographically dispersed locations within 10 minutes is physically impossible for a single human and is a definitive indicator of credential compromise. The immediate response is to delete the access key (iam:DeleteAccessKey), review CloudTrail for all API actions taken with the key, revoke any active sessions, and rotate any secrets or other credentials that may have been accessed during the compromise window. Waiting for more evidence while an active credential compromise is in progress allows the attacker to escalate privileges or exfiltrate data.

q: GuardDuty enabled for only 1 week. Already found 10 findings including high-severity. Is this unusual?
options: Only trust findings after 30 days
GuardDuty is broken
No; initial GuardDuty findings are often backlog (past 6-7 days reconstructed). Many are benign baseline activities. Triage by severity and recency
Yes, definitely under active attack
explain: When GuardDuty is first enabled, it processes up to 6-7 days of historical CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS data retroactively, which causes a rapid burst of findings in the first 24-72 hours that reflects historical patterns rather than a current active attack. Security teams should triage this initial volume by severity (HIGH first), distinguish one-time historical events from recurring patterns, and suppress known-good findings to establish a detection baseline. This initial volume spike is expected and does not indicate the account is under active attack.

q: Inspector scan found 5 CVEs on EC2 instance. 4 are library/dependency issues (can be patched). 1 is kernel-level (requires AMI update). How do you remediate efficiently?
options: Accept the CVEs
Terminate instance and launch new one
Patch kernel manually on each instance
Patch libraries on instance immediately, update AMI with patched OS, test in dev, roll out to ASG
explain: For a mixed EC2 CVE finding (library-level and kernel-level vulnerabilities), the most efficient approach patches the quick fixes (library updates) immediately on the running instance, then creates a new AMI with a patched OS for the kernel vulnerability, validates the new AMI in a development environment, and rolls it out via the Auto Scaling group. This approach addresses all vulnerabilities while minimizing downtime and ensuring future instances launched from the ASG use a clean AMI. Simply terminating the instance wastes the opportunity to patch and test systematically.

q: GuardDuty finding: UnauthorizedAccess:RDS/CryptoCurrencyDependencyExploit. What does this finding indicate?
options: This finding is not real
Cryptocurrency is attacking your database
RDS instance likely has a vulnerable application; investigate application code and patching
RDS database is mining cryptocurrency
explain: A GuardDuty CryptoCurrency:EC2/CryptoCurrencyDependencyExploit finding against an RDS instance indicates that a vulnerable application layer component (application code or dependency) is being exploited, not that the database itself is performing cryptomining. The investigation should focus on the application code connected to the RDS instance, checking for vulnerable libraries or unpatched application frameworks. RDS databases do not directly execute cryptocurrency mining software.

q: Detective investigation of a suspected EC2 compromise reveals 0 suspicious API calls in CloudTrail but high network traffic to unknown IPs in Flow Logs. What's happening?
options: Likely C2 communication or data exfiltration via non-AWS APIs; inspect network traffic, isolate instance, conduct forensics
Only CloudTrail matters
GuardDuty is wrong
Log the alert as a false positive without any investigation or escalation procedure
explain: When Detective shows high VPC Flow Log traffic to unknown IPs but no suspicious CloudTrail API calls, the most likely explanation is command-and-control (C2) communication or data exfiltration using non-AWS protocols that bypass CloudTrail (which only records AWS API calls). The investigation must focus on the network layer: isolate the instance to stop the communication, capture memory for forensic analysis, and analyze the full packet captures from VPC Flow Logs. Dismissing this as a CloudTrail gap or GuardDuty error ignores the clear network-layer evidence of suspicious outbound communication.

q: Your organization has auto-remediation Lambda for GuardDuty findings. The Lambda is deleting instances. Is this safe?
options: Deletion is fine
Risky; auto-deletion should only apply to non-production or those with automatic backup/recovery; require manual approval for production
Only delete instances without backups
Never use auto-remediation
explain: Auto-remediation that terminates EC2 instances is a high-risk action because it can cause irreversible data loss and service outages, particularly in production environments. The safe pattern is to apply auto-remediation (isolation, not termination) to non-production instances and require manual approval from the security team before terminating production instances. Backup and recovery procedures should be verified before any auto-termination is configured, and termination should be a last resort after isolation fails.

q: Macie is finding sensitive data in S3. Many findings are false positives (regular numbers detected as credit cards). How do you tune Macie?
options: Ignore all findings
Disable Amazon Macie to prevent it from generating findings that require investigation
Macie can't be tuned
Create custom data identifier to exclude false positive patterns, tune sensitivity settings, create custom suppression rules
explain: Amazon Macie supports custom data identifiers (regex-based patterns) to exclude false positive patterns that match benign data formats, and suppression rules that can archive findings matching specific criteria without requiring human review. Tuning these settings reduces noise volume while preserving detection sensitivity for genuinely risky patterns. Disabling Macie entirely eliminates all sensitive data visibility; ignoring findings defeats the purpose of enabling the service.

q: Inspector found critical CVE in production database instance (not part of ASG; singleton). How do you minimize downtime?
options: Accept the CVE as an acceptable risk given the internal nature of the affected service
Delete and recreate
Create read replica, patch replica first, test, failover to replica, patch original, failover back
Patch immediately (downtime)
explain: For patching a production singleton database without service interruption, the recommended approach is to create an RDS read replica, patch the replica (applying OS updates or RDS version upgrade), validate the patched replica's functionality, promote the replica to primary, and restore the original as a new standby. This eliminates downtime while ensuring the patched database is validated before serving production traffic. Patching in place with downtime is acceptable only when read replicas or multi-AZ failover are not available.

q: GuardDuty finding shows EC2 instance accessing suspicious domain. You block the domain in WAF. Will this fix the problem?
options: No; WAF only protects inbound to ALB/CloudFront, not outbound from EC2; isolate instance, check if data was exfiltrated, update security group rules
Blocking the domain fixes everything
Yes, WAF blocks all access
Outbound can't be blocked
explain: AWS WAF operates only on inbound HTTP/HTTPS traffic at the ALB or CloudFront layer and cannot block outbound connections from EC2 instances to external domains. For a GuardDuty finding showing an EC2 instance accessing a suspicious domain, the correct containment is to update the EC2 instance's outbound security group rules to remove the default allow-all-outbound rule, and to check VPC Flow Logs for evidence of data exfiltration before the finding. Security groups are stateful and control both inbound and outbound traffic at the instance level.

q: Detective reveals multiple IAM users from same account but different principals all accessing the same sensitive S3 bucket simultaneously. Is this normal?
options: Simultaneous access is always benign
Can't determine without context
Check context: if related to batch job or application, might be normal; if separate humans, investigate for coordinated access; review CloudTrail for actions taken
Definitely malicious
explain: Simultaneous multi-user S3 access from different IAM principals in the same account could be entirely normal if it corresponds to a batch job, automated data pipeline, or distributed application — all of which regularly access shared buckets concurrently. If the access involves separate human identities who would not normally work on the same data simultaneously, CloudTrail should be queried to understand what specific API actions (GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject) were taken and whether they align with expected workflows. Context is essential before treating concurrent access as a security incident.

q: You have GuardDuty enabled in 5 AWS regions. One region has 10x more findings than others. What might explain this?
options: Region might have more resources, different workload type, or actually under attack; compare resource counts, check if findings are actionable
GuardDuty is broken in that region
All regions should have same findings
Disable GuardDuty in that region
explain: Higher GuardDuty finding volumes in one region are almost always explained by resource density differences (more EC2 instances, more Lambda functions, more S3 bucket activity) or workload type differences (batch processing generating more API calls). The response is to compare the finding-to-resource ratio across regions, not the raw finding count. If the ratio is significantly higher, the region warrants deeper investigation; if it matches the resource count, the volume is expected.

q: Inspector detects EOL (end-of-life) software on an EC2 instance. The software is no longer patched but still functional. Should you replace it?
options: EOL isn't a security concern
Never update software
EOL is fine if it works
Yes; EOL software won't receive security patches, increasing breach risk; prioritize based on exposure (if internet-facing, higher priority)
explain: End-of-life software no longer receives security patches from the vendor, meaning each newly discovered CVE in that software becomes a permanent, unmitigated vulnerability. The business risk assessment depends on the software's internet exposure: internet-facing EOL software should be prioritized for immediate replacement, while internal-only EOL software with network controls can be deferred with compensating controls documented. Accepting EOL software as safe because it is functional ignores the cumulative risk of unpatched vulnerabilities.

q: GuardDuty lateral movement detection across EC2, RDS, and Lambda reveals attack path. Detective entity profiles identify which services were targeted.
options: Check only RDS logs without EC2 context
Review logs manually without Detective
Use Detective's investigation graphs to map attack timeline and identify compromised resources for isolation
Assume only EC2 was compromised
explain: Amazon Detective's investigation graphs provide a visual representation of how an attack moved laterally across EC2, RDS, and Lambda services by correlating GuardDuty findings with CloudTrail API calls and VPC Flow Logs across all affected entities. Using Detective's entity profiles, investigators can identify which resources were accessed, in what sequence, and from what source identity, dramatically reducing the time to understand the full scope of a multi-service compromise. Reviewing each service's logs independently loses the correlation context that Detective provides.

q: GuardDuty suppression rules filter findings from known security assessments. Rules are created based on expected testing. How should you manage rule lifecycle?
options: Keep all rules indefinitely
Delete all rules to see all findings
Set suppression rules once and never review
Review quarterly; remove obsolete rules based on current assessment schedule and infrastructure changes
explain: GuardDuty suppression rules for known security assessments should be reviewed on a quarterly basis to confirm that each suppression rule still corresponds to an active, scheduled assessment and that the assessment scope has not changed. Infrastructure changes (new IP ranges, new resource types) may invalidate existing suppression rules, causing them to suppress real threats. Rules that are never reviewed tend to accumulate over time and create blind spots.

q: Inspector identifies critical (CVSS 9.8) kernel vulnerability on stateless EC2 in Auto Scaling group behind load balancer.
options: Accept risk without patching
Launch patched AMI, update target group, terminate vulnerable instance for zero-downtime remediation
Manually patch without reboot
Use WAF to mitigate kernel vuln
explain: For a stateless EC2 instance in an Auto Scaling group with a CVSS 9.8 kernel vulnerability, the zero-downtime remediation process is to build a patched AMI with the updated kernel, update the ASG launch template to reference the new AMI, and then either trigger an instance refresh or gradually replace running instances by terminating old ones while the ASG launches patched replacements. AWS WAF cannot mitigate kernel-level vulnerabilities because it operates at the HTTP layer. Manual patching without a reboot cannot apply kernel updates.

q: Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, IAM Access Analyzer across accounts. Team overwhelmed by volume.
options: Review all findings manually without prioritization
Disable less critical findings sources
Export to spreadsheets weekly
Create custom insights filtering by severity, resource type, and age; route to incident response workflow
explain: When Security Hub receives hundreds of findings daily from GuardDuty, Inspector, and IAM Access Analyzer, custom insights with filters for severity (HIGH/CRITICAL), resource type, finding age, and workflow status allow teams to create prioritized work queues without manually reviewing every finding. Routing high-priority filtered findings to PagerDuty via EventBridge enables on-call engineers to receive only actionable alerts. Disabling data sources removes visibility; exporting to spreadsheets weekly is too slow for active threat response.

q: Macie discovers unencrypted PII (SSN, credit card) in S3 bucket readable by data team. Urgent remediation required.
options: Enable default S3 encryption with KMS, restrict IAM policy, quarantine unencrypted objects for review
Use CloudFront distribution
Make bucket private but leave objects unencrypted
Delete bucket immediately
explain: When Macie discovers unencrypted PII in an S3 bucket accessible by the data team, the correct immediate actions are: apply SSE-KMS default encryption (protecting future uploads), restrict the IAM policy to limit access to only authorized data team members, and quarantine the existing unencrypted objects for review before deleting them. Making the bucket private without encrypting objects leaves existing data vulnerable to anyone who already has the AWS credentials. Deleting the bucket immediately may destroy data needed for compliance or business purposes.

q: Detective detects brute-force pattern: 15 staging EC2 instances with repeated SSH failures from same external IP. Immediate action?
options: CloudWatch monitoring issue
Normal behavior not requiring investigation
Revoke SSH access, review IAM credentials for automation, investigate source IP and access logs
Misconfigured load balancer causing failures
explain: Repeated SSH failures from the same source IP across 15 staging EC2 instances in Detective's investigation graphs indicates a systematic brute-force attack targeting the entire staging environment. The immediate response should revoke the SSH security group rules, review CloudTrail for any successful authentication attempts, and investigate whether the source IP corresponds to an authorized security assessment. CloudWatch metrics alone cannot explain why SSH failures are occurring on multiple hosts from a single source.

q: GuardDuty CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool finding on instance in Auto Scaling group. EventBridge routes to Lambda remediation.
options: Terminate instance, scan source AMI with Inspector, notify security, review ASG image
Continue normal operation
Add instance to load balancer
Ignore if instance less than 1 hour old
explain: For a confirmed CryptoCurrency finding on an ASG instance, EventBridge should route the GuardDuty finding to a Lambda function that terminates the compromised instance (allowing the ASG to launch a replacement), triggers an Inspector scan of the source AMI used to launch the instance, and notifies the security team. The source AMI must be investigated because if the malware was embedded in the AMI, all future ASG instances will also be compromised. Simply relaunching without scanning the AMI perpetuates the infection.

q: Inspector ECR image scan detects critical library vulnerability (application layer dependency outdated). Remediation approach?
options: Ignore third-party vulnerabilities
Use WAF to mitigate container vuln
Patch at runtime during execution
Update library dependency, rebuild image, push to ECR, update task definition, redeploy
explain: Remediating a critical library vulnerability in an ECR container image requires updating the dependency in the application's requirements file or package manifest, rebuilding the container image, pushing the new image to ECR, updating the ECS or EKS task/pod definition to reference the new image tag, and redeploying the service. Container vulnerabilities exist in immutable image layers and cannot be patched at runtime. AWS WAF cannot mitigate application-layer vulnerabilities inside container images.

q: CloudTrail organization trail with log file validation ensures integrity. Multi-region replication provides durability. Implement for compliance?
options: Separate regional trails without validation
CloudWatch Logs only without S3
Organization trail + S3 replication + log file validation enabled
Single region without replication
explain: An organization CloudTrail trail with log file validation enabled provides tamper-evident hashing (SHA-256 HMAC) for every log file, and S3 Cross-Region Replication ensures logs survive regional outages or deletion attempts in the primary bucket. Together, these satisfy compliance requirements for integrity verification and durability. Separate regional trails without validation provide no integrity assurance, and CloudWatch Logs alone does not provide the long-term, tamper-evident storage that compliance frameworks require.

q: CloudTrail Insights detects 3x spike in DeleteObject API calls at 2 AM UTC. Investigation should identify IAM principal and automation jobs.
options: Assume false positive and ignore
Only check S3 access logs
Query CloudTrail for DeleteObject events, cross-reference IAM, review automation
Immediately delete all buckets
explain: CloudTrail Insights detected a 3x spike in DeleteObject API calls, which could indicate legitimate automation (scheduled cleanup jobs), authorized bulk deletion, or unauthorized data destruction. The investigation must identify the IAM principal from CloudTrail's userIdentity field, cross-reference with known automation job schedules, and verify whether the volume of deletions was expected. Acting without investigation (such as deleting all buckets) destroys evidence and causes additional damage.

q: VPC Flow Logs shows REJECT port 22 from internal subnet but CloudTrail shows no SSH attempts. What does this indicate?
options: Critical breach occurring
VPC Flow Logs unreliable
Network layer controls (SG/NACL) are successfully blocking as designed
CloudTrail missing data
explain: VPC Flow Logs recording REJECT status for port 22 from an internal subnet indicates that the security group or Network ACL is working as designed — the rule to block SSH is actively preventing connections. The absence of corresponding CloudTrail entries confirms no successful SSH session was established, which is the expected behavior. This is defense-in-depth functioning correctly, not a security incident.

q: RDS authentication failures not captured by CloudTrail (API-level only). Monitor database layer with what service?
options: Use VPC Flow Logs for database
Query database directly hourly
CloudTrail data events for RDS API
RDS Enhanced Monitoring sending logs to CloudWatch Logs with metric filters
explain: CloudTrail only captures AWS API-level events (RDS API calls like CreateDBInstance, ModifyDBInstance) and does not record database-level authentication events like failed login attempts to MySQL or PostgreSQL. RDS Enhanced Monitoring combined with database-level audit logs (such as MySQL General Log or PostgreSQL log_connections) delivered to CloudWatch Logs provides the authentication-layer monitoring required. A CloudWatch Logs metric filter on database error messages can then trigger alarms on authentication failure spikes.

q: CloudTrail management events enabled. S3 object modifications not appearing in logs. Enable data events for what?
options: CloudTrail S3 data events logging GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject
AWS Config object change tracking
VPC Flow Logs for S3 traffic
CloudWatch Logs instead of CloudTrail
explain: CloudTrail management events record service-level API calls (CreateBucket, RunInstances) but do not record S3 object-level operations (GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject) by default. S3 data events must be explicitly enabled in a CloudTrail trail to capture object-level operations; these are billed separately per 100,000 events. CloudWatch Logs and AWS Config do not capture S3 object-level operations in the same way as CloudTrail data events.

q: CloudTrail logs transition to Glacier after 30 days. Compliance requires 7-year retention. Cost-optimized approach?
options: Lifecycle: Standard→Glacier→Deep Archive by timespan, S3 Object Lock 7-year retention
CloudTrail auto-retains
Keep all in Standard indefinitely
Delete after 30 days
explain: S3 Lifecycle policies provide automated, cost-optimized tiering: CloudTrail logs move from Standard to S3 Glacier after 30 days (reducing storage cost by approximately 75%), then to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 90 days (reducing cost by an additional 50%). S3 Object Lock in compliance mode enforces immutable retention for the full 7-year period, preventing deletion or overwriting by any IAM principal including root. This combination satisfies both cost optimization and regulatory retention requirements.

q: Lambda parses CloudTrail JSON logs from CloudWatch subscription filter for alerting. What format enables efficient parsing?
options: AWS Config format
CSV format (not supported)
CloudTrail JSON format with userIdentity, eventName, requestParameters extraction
Raw text format
explain: CloudTrail logs are delivered in JSON format with well-defined fields (userIdentity, eventName, requestParameters, sourceIPAddress) that Lambda functions can parse using standard JSON libraries to extract, filter, and route specific event types. This structured format enables precise metric filters in CloudWatch Logs and efficient programmatic processing. CSV and raw text formats would require custom parsing logic that is error-prone and less performant.

q: Route 53 Resolver DNS logging detects queries to malicious dynamic DNS providers (C&C). Automate detection how?
options: Manual daily log review
Disable logging to reduce noise
Assume DNS queries harmless
CloudWatch metric filter + EventBridge trigger to Lambda for automated response
explain: Route 53 Resolver DNS query logging records all DNS queries made by resources within a VPC, including queries to command-and-control domains. Routing these logs through a CloudWatch metric filter detects query patterns matching known malicious domain patterns, and an EventBridge rule can trigger a Lambda function to block the domain in Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall and quarantine the querying instance. Manual daily log review introduces unacceptable latency for active C2 communication.

q: AWS Config timeline shows SG port 3306 open to 0.0.0.0/0 from 2-4 PM. RDS accessed from external IPs during window. Investigate by?
options: Delete SG immediately
CloudTrail RDS API calls + RDS Enhanced Monitoring for auth attempts during time window
Only check Config without other logs
Assume no unauthorized access
explain: AWS Config configuration history provides a timeline of when security group rules changed, including the specific rule that opened port 3306 to 0.0.0.0/0. This must be correlated with CloudTrail to identify the IAM principal that made the change, and with RDS Enhanced Monitoring logs to determine whether authentication attempts occurred during the exposure window. Using only Config without other log sources cannot answer whether unauthorized access actually succeeded.

q: S3 access logs show spike in GETs from unknown IP. Determine which sensitive objects were accessed using what?
options: Only VPC Flow Logs
S3 access logs for requests + CloudTrail GetObject events for IAM identity
Disable S3 logging
Assume IP is CDN
explain: S3 server access logs record every HTTP request made to a bucket with the requester's IP address, object key, and HTTP status code, while CloudTrail S3 data events capture the same operations with the IAM identity (ARN) that authenticated the request. Using both sources together enables complete forensic reconstruction: the server access log shows what was accessed and from which IP, while CloudTrail identifies the authenticated AWS identity. VPC Flow Logs show network connections but not the specific S3 objects accessed.

q: CloudTrail log file validation check failed for 3-4 PM logs. What does failure indicate and what action?
options: Validation failures are normal
Logs tampered with or deleted; immediately quarantine and begin incident investigation
Re-enable validation to fix
CloudTrail prevents tampering automatically
explain: CloudTrail log file validation failure means the cryptographic hash of the log file does not match the digest file generated at log delivery time, indicating that either the log file was modified after delivery or was deleted and replaced. This is a potential security incident requiring immediate investigation into who had S3 write access to the logging bucket during the affected time window. Validation failures are not normal — they indicate tampering or infrastructure-level compromise.

q: VPC Flow Logs in CloudWatch Logs with 30-day retention, no S3 export. Incident investigation needs 60-day logs. Problem?
options: VPC Flow Logs auto-archive to S3
30-day policy deletes logs; implement S3 export with Lifecycle for long-term retention
Only recent logs matter
CloudWatch retains indefinitely
explain: CloudWatch Logs has configurable retention policies that automatically delete log events after the specified retention period, and 30-day retention means 60-day-old VPC Flow Logs no longer exist in CloudWatch. The correct solution is to export VPC Flow Logs to S3 in parallel with CloudWatch delivery, and apply an S3 Lifecycle policy to retain logs for the full 60+ days required. CloudWatch does not automatically archive to S3 before deletion.

q: CloudTrail data events log 100K Lambda invokes/hour creating massive costs. Optimize without losing audit trail by?
options: Use exclude filters for trusted high-volume invokes, log external/untrusted principals
Disable data event logging entirely
CloudWatch Logs without CloudTrail
Accept high costs
explain: CloudTrail data event logging at high API call volumes generates costs proportional to the number of recorded events. Using advanced event selectors to exclude high-volume, low-risk Lambda invocations (such as internal health checks from trusted automation) while retaining logging for external or untrusted principals maintains a meaningful audit trail at a fraction of the cost. Amazon Athena with partition projection then queries only relevant log partitions, further reducing query costs.

q: AWS Config aggregator shows no data from member account. CloudTrail shows Config API events. Authorization missing?
options: Member account must authorize aggregator via GetAggregationAuthorization, aggregator IAM role needs cross-account permissions
All accounts auto-share data
Recreate aggregator
Use per-account Config only
explain: AWS Config aggregators collect configuration and compliance data from member accounts through explicit cross-account authorization — the member account must grant the aggregator account permission to collect its data using an aggregation authorization resource or through AWS Organizations service-linked roles. If CloudTrail confirms Config is running in the member account but the aggregator shows no data, the most likely cause is missing aggregation authorization or an IAM permission preventing Config from delivering data to the central account.

q: CloudTrail logs in Glacier per Lifecycle policy. Incident investigation needs 30-day-old logs. Retrieval impact?
options: Immediately available
Standard retrieval 3-5 hours; consider Intelligent-Tiering or expedited retrieval for forensics
Auto-restore without delay
Impossible to retrieve
explain: S3 Glacier Standard retrieval takes 3-5 hours per request, which can significantly delay incident response investigations requiring 30-day-old CloudTrail logs. For security investigations requiring fast access to archived data, Glacier Expedited retrieval completes in 1-5 minutes at higher cost, or Glacier Instant Retrieval storage class provides millisecond access for frequently needed archived data. Incident response plans should document which retrieval tier to use for time-sensitive forensic investigations.

q: Compliance: CloudTrail logs immutable 7 years, accessible 30 days, archived for cost. Implementation?
options: CloudTrail auto-retains
Delete after 30 days
S3 Object Lock compliance mode 7yr, Lifecycle Standard→Glacier→Deep Archive, log file validation
Keep all Standard indefinitely
explain: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode prevents deletion or overwriting of any object version for the entire retention period, even by the account root user or AWS Support, satisfying tamper-proof retention requirements. Combining a Lifecycle policy that transitions logs through Standard, Glacier, and Deep Archive tiers with Object Lock compliance mode and CloudTrail log file validation provides both cost optimization and immutability. AWS CloudTrail does not manage its own long-term retention; S3 lifecycle management is required.

q: Central logging: 100 accounts send CloudTrail to security account S3. Prevent unauthorized cross-account access by?
options: Assume CloudTrail logs always private
S3 bucket policy allows only CloudTrail service principal and security account principals
VPC endpoints only without policies
Make bucket public
explain: A central S3 logging bucket receiving CloudTrail from 100 member accounts requires an explicit bucket policy allowing CloudTrail's service principal (delivery.logs.amazonaws.com) to perform PutObject, scoped to each member account using an aws:SourceAccount or aws:SourceOrgID condition. Without this cross-account grant, CloudTrail cannot deliver logs from member accounts to the security account bucket because S3 bucket policies default to deny for cross-account operations. Making the bucket public would expose audit logs to the internet.

q: AWS WAF managed rule detects SQL injection. New zero-day variant bypasses detection. Respond by?
options: Disable WAF entirely
Add custom rule for new pattern, set Block, monitor AWS Managed Rules updates
Block all HTTP requests
Assume WAF perfect and needs no enhancement
explain: AWS WAF custom rules using SQL injection match conditions inspect request content (query strings, URI, body, headers) for SQL injection patterns and block them regardless of source IP, making them effective against IP-rotating attacks. Updating custom rules alongside the regularly maintained AWS Managed Rules for SQL database provides defense-in-depth. Disabling WAF entirely removes the primary application-layer defense, and blocking all HTTP requests makes the application unavailable.

q: Sustained DDoS attack on CloudFront (10K+ IPs). Have Shield Advanced with DRT. Immediate actions?
options: Disable CloudFront (breaks app)
Accept the current security posture and document the finding as a known acceptable risk
Manually block individual IPs
Contact AWS DRT, enable WAF rate-limiting, verify cost protection enabled
explain: During an active DDoS attack with 10,000+ source IPs, the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT) — accessible to Shield Advanced customers — can engage directly to write custom WAF rules, adjust Shield mitigations, and implement rate-based rules that distinguish legitimate traffic from attack traffic at the CloudFront edge. IP-based blocking is impractical at this scale and attackers can trivially rotate IPs. Disabling CloudFront removes the DDoS protection layer and takes the application offline.

q: Network Firewall across 3 VPCs in inspection mode. Detect data exfiltration to C&C domains using what rules?
options: Stateful Suricata rules inspecting HTTPS SNI and DNS for malicious domains
IP-based rules only
Manual connection review
Stateless rules (no session context)
explain: AWS Network Firewall's stateful Suricata-compatible rules inspect packet content at the application layer, including HTTPS Server Name Indication (SNI) fields and DNS query names, enabling detection of connections to known command-and-control domains even over encrypted traffic. Stateless rules only inspect packet headers (source IP, destination port, protocol) without examining application-layer content, making them inadequate for domain-based exfiltration detection. Manual connection review cannot scale to the volume of traffic in a production environment.

q: SG allows SSH (22) from 0.0.0.0/0. NACL has explicit DENY port 22 inbound. SSH succeeds?
options: No, NACL evaluated first, stateless DENY blocks traffic at network layer
Maybe, depends on IP
Restrict all VPC traffic to IPv6-only to prevent the security group rule from applying to IPv4 SSH
Yes, SG overrides NACL
explain: Network ACLs are evaluated before security groups in AWS's traffic processing pipeline. A NACL's explicit DENY rule for port 22 terminates traffic processing before the security group is ever evaluated, so even a security group with an allow rule for SSH from 0.0.0.0/0 cannot override the NACL deny. NACLs are stateless, so both inbound and outbound rules must be configured, whereas security groups are stateful and automatically allow return traffic.

q: VPC endpoint S3 policy allows specific IAM roles GetObject. Users still access S3 via internet. Enforce endpoint-only by?
options: S3 bucket policy condition 'aws:sourceVpce' restricts to endpoint, or SCP denies S3 unless sourceVpce specified
Endpoint policies auto-enforce globally (not true)
Use NACLs without bucket policy
Remove internet gateway
explain: VPC endpoint policies define what API actions can be performed through the endpoint, but they do not prevent resources from accessing S3 via the internet gateway if one exists. To enforce endpoint-only S3 access, the S3 bucket policy must include an aws:sourceVpce condition that denies requests not originating from the specific VPC endpoint, or an SCP can deny S3 actions when the aws:sourceVpce condition is not present. These two controls together create a complete enforcement boundary.

q: Developer needs SSH for troubleshooting without managing keys. Audit trail required for all commands. Use what?
options: Store private keys in home directory
Use insecure telnet
AWS Systems Manager Session Manager with IAM auth; logs commands to CloudTrail and CloudWatch
Share EC2 key pairs
explain: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based and CLI-based shell access to EC2 instances using IAM authentication, eliminating the need for SSH key pairs and open port 22. Every session command and output is logged to CloudWatch Logs and S3, and each session event is recorded in CloudTrail, providing a complete audit trail. SSH key sharing creates long-term credential exposure risks and does not provide command-level audit trails.

q: Zero-trust architecture: Internal apps require user auth before access regardless of network location. Implement using?
options: No security controls
AWS Verified Access with identity provider, device compliance, continuous trust evaluation
VPN without additional auth
Internet access + WAF only
explain: AWS Verified Access implements zero-trust access by evaluating identity (from an IdP like Okta or IAM Identity Center), device compliance (via AWS Verified Access trust providers), and network context before granting access to each internal application. Unlike traditional VPNs that grant broad network access after authentication, Verified Access provides per-request, per-application access decisions with continuous trust evaluation. WAF only protects HTTP traffic at the edge and does not enforce identity-based access for all internal users.

q: ALB wildcard cert expiring 30 days. ACM auto-renewal fails due to deleted DNS validation records. Solution?
options: Use self-signed certificates
Manually add Route 53 DNS validation records, prevent future deletion via policy
Wait for expiration then recreate
Assume ACM renewal always succeeds
explain: ACM certificate auto-renewal works by automatically adding or updating the CNAME DNS validation record in Route 53. If those validation records were deleted, ACM cannot complete the domain validation step and the renewal fails. The fix is to manually add the required DNS CNAME validation records back to Route 53, then verify ACM completes the renewal successfully. To prevent future failures, an SCP or Route 53 resource policy should restrict deletion of the validation records.

q: WAF rate-limiting (100 reqs/5 min per IP) blocks legitimate mobile app via proxy. Fix by?
options: Assume rule is perfect
Disable rate-limiting entirely
Block all mobile traffic
Whitelist corporate proxy IP or change scope to X-Forwarded-For header
explain: WAF rate-limiting rules operate by counting requests per source IP within a time window, so all users behind a corporate proxy share the same source IP and are collectively rate-limited as if they were one requester. The fix is to configure the rate-limiting rule to scope on the X-Forwarded-For header (which carries the individual user's real IP) rather than the source IP, or to whitelist the known corporate proxy IP from rate-limiting rules. Disabling rate-limiting entirely removes protection against DoS attacks.

q: Network Firewall DOMAIN_LIST denies C&C domains. Attacker uses DNS tunneling to exfiltrate data. Detect by?
options: Stateful rules detecting DNS pattern anomalies (long responses, high frequency)
Domain blocking only
Block all outbound DNS resolution from the VPC to prevent data exfiltration via DNS tunneling
DNS tunneling undetectable
explain: DNS tunneling embeds data in DNS query subdomains (e.g., a3f92b.data.attacker.com) to exfiltrate data through DNS resolution, bypassing domain blocklists if the tunnel domain has not yet been classified as malicious. Detecting DNS tunneling requires stateful pattern analysis: unusually long subdomain labels, high-entropy subdomain names, abnormally high query frequency, and large DNS response sizes are all indicators. Blocking all outbound DNS would break normal name resolution for the entire VPC.

q: Microservices require mTLS with ACM Private CA certs. Connections rejected. Configuration issue?
options: Use self-signed without CA
Certs require no validation
mTLS not supported in AWS (false)
Services not validating peer certs or trusting CA root; ensure chains installed and mTLS enabled
explain: Mutual TLS (mTLS) requires both the client and server to present valid certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority and to validate each other's certificates. If services are rejecting connections despite having ACM Private CA certificates installed, the most likely causes are: trust chain not fully installed (intermediate CA certs missing), mTLS not enabled in the service configuration, or peer certificate validation disabled. Using self-signed certificates without a CA bypasses the certificate chain validation that mTLS requires.

q: SG allows SSH (22), NACL has explicit DENY 22 from 0.0.0.0/0. IP 203.0.113.0 attempts SSH. Result?
options: Depends on NACL rule order
SG and NACL equal priority
Succeeds due to SG allow
Denied by NACL (stateless, evaluated first)
explain: Network ACLs evaluate rules in ascending rule number order, and the first matching rule determines the action — subsequent rules are not evaluated. An explicit NACL DENY rule for port 22 is processed before the security group is ever reached, because NACLs are evaluated at the subnet boundary before traffic reaches the instance where security groups apply. The security group's allow rule is irrelevant when the NACL has already denied the traffic.

q: NLB behind WAF, CloudFront in front. Attacker targets NLB IP directly bypassing CloudFront. Prevent by?
options: WAF at origin only (redundant)
Origin Shield, restrict SG to CloudFront IPs, Network Firewall block direct origin IPs
CloudFront always intercepts (not for direct IPs)
Move NLB to private subnet (breaks traffic)
explain: When CloudFront is placed in front of an NLB for WAF protection, the NLB's public IP and DNS name remain directly accessible, allowing attackers to bypass CloudFront and reach the origin unfiltered. The defense requires two layers: restrict the NLB's security group to only allow inbound traffic from CloudFront's managed IP prefix list (ensuring all internet traffic goes through CloudFront's WAF), and optionally use CloudFront custom headers that the origin validates, blocking requests that don't include the expected header. AWS PrivateLink can further restrict origin access to private connectivity.

q: WebACL: Rule priority 1 ALLOWs, priority 2 DENYs same traffic. Request matches both. Precedence?
options: Both evaluated and combined
Highest priority (1) ALLOW evaluated first; matching rule terminates processing
DENY always overrides ALLOW
Depends on rule group type
explain: AWS WAF evaluates rules in ascending priority order (lowest number first), and the first matching rule terminates evaluation — the request is assigned the matching rule's action without evaluating subsequent rules. If Rule 1 (priority 1) ALLOWs traffic and Rule 2 (priority 2) DENYs the same traffic, requests matching both rules are ALLOWed because the higher-priority (lower number) ALLOW rule is evaluated first. To change this behavior, the DENY rule must be assigned a lower priority number than the ALLOW rule.

q: Suricata rule SID 123456 causes false positives on legitimate traffic. Reduce false positives by?
options: Ignore false positives
Disable entire rule group
Disable SID 123456 selectively or use rule group bypass actions for legitimate patterns
Manual review of every alert
explain: AWS Network Firewall Suricata-compatible rules can be selectively suppressed by adding the specific SID to a suppression list within the rule group, preventing that rule from generating alerts or taking action on matching traffic while leaving all other rules active. This granular suppression capability allows security teams to reduce false positive noise without disabling entire rule groups. Manually reviewing every alert is not operationally scalable for high-traffic environments.

q: Mobile app implements certificate pinning to prevent MITM. ACM cert rotates unexpectedly. Impact and mitigation?
options: Pinning auto-updates (not true)
Pinning prevents rotation
Rotation has no impact
App can't connect; use Certificate Transparency logs to monitor changes, implement dynamic pinning with backup certs
explain: Certificate pinning hardcodes a specific certificate's public key or fingerprint into the application client. When ACM rotates the certificate unexpectedly, the new certificate's public key does not match the pinned value, causing the TLS handshake to fail and the application to be unable to connect. The mitigation is to implement dynamic pinning with backup certificates (pin to the CA's public key rather than the leaf certificate) and monitor Certificate Transparency logs to receive advance notice of certificate changes before rotation completes.

q: ALB in public subnet with WAF. SCP denies SG modification for organization. Developer wants corporate IP SSH. How to allow?
options: SCPs don't apply to SG management
Developers can modify if persistent
Use NACLs instead to bypass SCP
Create IAM role with specific SG modify permissions, or request SCP exception for dev OUs
explain: Service Control Policies (SCPs) apply to all principals in an account including administrators, and restricting security group modification via SCP means even admin-level IAM actions are blocked. The correct approach for legitimate exceptions is to create an IAM role with specifically scoped SG modification permissions (limited to the specific security group and CIDR) and submit a request to modify the SCP to allow this role's actions, or work with the organization security team to create an SCP exception for the development OU. NACLs are separate from security groups and modifying NACLs does not bypass the SCP restriction on security groups.

q: CloudFront HTTPS only (HTTP redirected). WAF blocks suspicious User-Agent. App has legitimate unusual User-Agent. Whitelist by?
options: Create WAF rule exception for app's User-Agent or exclude from WAF rule evaluation
Disable HTTPS requirement (security risk)
Block all User-Agent headers
Assume WAF perfect
explain: AWS WAF rules can be configured with exclusion statements that prevent specific request attributes from triggering a rule, allowing the rule to continue blocking other requests that match. For a legitimate application with an unusual User-Agent that triggers a managed rule false positive, the correct fix is to add a scope-down statement or exclusion that whitelists the known application's User-Agent string while preserving the rule's detection for unknown User-Agents. Disabling the HTTPS redirect requirement is a security regression.

q: NLB with cross-zone load balancing and NAT gateway. Backend IP whitelist expects all traffic from NAT IP. Issue?
options: Cross-zone doesn't affect source IP
IP whitelisting always sufficient
Single NAT gateway per cross-zone
Cross-zone uses multiple NAT gateway IPs; peer all or use SG for broader whitelisting
explain: Network Load Balancers preserve the original source IP of clients and do not use NAT gateways for load balancing. When cross-zone load balancing is enabled, the NLB routes traffic across AZs, but each AZ has its own ENI with a distinct IP address. If backend services whitelist only one expected source IP, requests routed through other AZ ENIs will be rejected. The fix is to whitelist all NLB private IP addresses in each AZ, or use security group references instead of IP-based whitelisting.

q: Application layer rate-limiting + WAF layer rate-limiting implemented. Duplicate limiting cause issues?
options: Duplicate limiting causes conflicts
Both work independently; WAF faster at edge, app provides defense-in-depth if WAF bypassed
App rate-limiting redundant
Only WAF rate-limiting needed
explain: Layered rate-limiting provides defense-in-depth: AWS WAF rate-based rules block volumetric attacks at the edge before they reach the application, while application-layer rate-limiting provides a secondary defense for WAF bypass attempts and fine-grained per-user or per-endpoint throttling. The two layers operate independently and do not conflict — they both enforce limits on different threat vectors. Removing either layer creates a potential bypass path.

q: Developer assumes role with EC2:TerminateInstances perm. Trust policy requires MFA + external ID. Assume without MFA?
options: AssumeRole denied by trust policy MFA requirement before assumption completes
Allowed if external ID provided
Developer assumes and can terminate
MFA checked after assumption
explain: IAM role trust policies are evaluated during the AssumeRole API call before the role's permission policy is checked. When the trust policy includes a condition requiring aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent = true, AssumeRole returns an AccessDenied error if the caller's session was not authenticated with MFA — regardless of what permissions the calling user's IAM policy grants. This ensures high-privilege roles (such as those with EC2:TerminateInstances) can only be assumed by verified, MFA-authenticated identities.

q: IAM user: inline allow S3:GetObject + explicit deny same action. User attempts read. Result?
options: Allow takes precedence
Succeeds with MFA
Denied; explicit deny highest priority overrides any allow
Depends on policy order
explain: Explicit denies in AWS IAM are the highest priority evaluation outcome and override any Allow from any source — inline policies, managed policies, resource-based policies, or permission boundaries. When an IAM user has both an inline allow for S3:GetObject and an explicit deny for the same action (from any policy source), the explicit deny wins and the user cannot perform the action. Policy evaluation order does not change this behavior; explicit denies always take precedence.

q: Permission boundary denies S3 access. Developer creates role with AdministratorAccess. Can role access S3?
options: Yes, AdministratorAccess overrides boundaries
No, boundary is maximum permission set; cannot exceed
Yes, the developer can assume the role even without MFA if they use the AWS console login flow
Depends on resource-based policies
explain: IAM permission boundaries define the maximum set of permissions an IAM entity can have, regardless of what is granted in its identity-based policies. Even if a developer creates a role with AdministratorAccess, the role's effective permissions are the intersection of AdministratorAccess and the permission boundary — if the boundary denies S3 access, the role cannot access S3. Permission boundaries cannot be overridden by attaching additional permission policies.

q: AWS IAM Identity Center manages 50 accounts. Marketing needs S3 + CloudWatch without per-account users. Approach?
options: Identity Center can't scale
Cross-account roles without Identity Center
Permission set for marketing group assigned to OUs containing 50 accounts
Individual IAM users per account
explain: AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets assigned to groups can be applied to multiple accounts simultaneously, allowing a marketing group to access all 50 accounts with the appropriate S3 and CloudWatch permissions without creating individual IAM users in each account. Assigning the permission set to the organizational unit containing the 50 accounts further automates deployment of new accounts. Individual IAM users per account require N-times the management overhead and create identity sprawl.

q: Third-party vendor from different account needs S3 access to specific buckets. Prevent escalation by?
