options: Share root credentials (insecure)
Cross-account role with external ID in trust policy, restrict to specific bucket ARNs
Only resource-based policies
Assume cross-account access insecure (not with external ID)
explain: Cross-account IAM roles with an external ID in the trust policy protect against confused deputy attacks where an attacker tricks a service into assuming a role on their behalf. The external ID ensures only the specific vendor (who knows the external ID) can assume the role, and restricting the role to specific bucket ARNs implements least privilege for the cross-account access. Sharing root credentials grants unlimited account access and is a critical security violation.

q: STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity for Google login. Attacker crafts malicious token. Prevent by?
options: Accept any Google token (insecure)
Store tokens in CloudTrail
Disable web identity federation entirely
Validate token 'aud' (audience) claim in trust policy to restrict to your app, validate issuer
explain: AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity validates the JSON Web Token (JWT) from the identity provider, but the trust policy must verify the token's audience (aud) claim to ensure the token was issued for your specific application, not just any Google login. Without audience validation, any Google user can construct a valid token for a different application and use it to assume the AWS role. The trust policy condition keys sts:AuditorId or oidc:aud enforce this verification.

q: ABAC uses tags (Department:Engineering, CostCenter:1000). Attacker gains user creds but can't add tags (iam:TagUser denied). Escalate?
options: No, session tags immutable during session; can't exceed tagged permissions
Only IAM policies matter
Tags don't provide security
Yes, attacker adds tags to self
explain: Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) with session tags is secure when iam:TagUser is explicitly denied, because session tags are set at AssumeRole time and cannot be modified during the active session. An attacker with compromised credentials cannot escalate privileges by adding tags to themselves because the iam:TagUser permission is denied, and session tags are immutable for the duration of the session. ABAC only provides the security guarantee described when tag modification permissions are properly locked down.

q: SLR created for EC2. Organization SCP denies all ec2:* actions. Resolve conflict by?
options: This is a known AWS Suricata bug that cannot be fixed and requires accepting the false positives
SLRs don't exist (false)
SCP doesn't restrict SLR permissions (granted via service trust); adjust SCP for non-SLR EC2 or exempt SLR
SCPs always restrict SLRs
explain: Service-linked roles are created and managed by AWS services and receive permissions through the service's own trust policy, not through IAM permission policies. SCPs restrict what IAM principals can do, but they do not restrict actions taken by AWS service-linked roles because SLR permissions are granted through AWS's internal service trust model. If an SCP denies all ec2:* actions and an SLR needs EC2 permissions, the correct solution is to update the SCP to exclude SLR-sourced calls or to scope the SCP with a condition that exempts SLRs.

q: IAM Access Analyzer reports external account (111111111111) has bucket read access. This is authorized vendor. Manage by?
options: Assume finding false positive
Verify vendor account ID, annotate finding, implement periodic re-validation of trusted external principal
Remove all external access (breaks vendor)
Delete bucket policy
explain: IAM Access Analyzer continuously monitors resource-based policies to identify resources shared with external entities (cross-account access or public access). When an external account is identified and confirmed as an authorized vendor, the correct practice is to annotate the finding as a known exception in Access Analyzer, document the business justification, and schedule periodic re-validation to confirm the authorization remains current. Removing all external access would break the vendor workflow; assuming the finding is a false positive without verification is a security risk.

q: User: inline allow iam:CreateAccessKey + iam:CreateUser. Session policy restricts to iam:CreateAccessKey. Can do what?
options: CreateUser with MFA
Both CreateAccessKey and CreateUser
Take no action since the security group and NACL controls are independent and do not interact
Only CreateAccessKey (session policy is intersection with user perms)
explain: Session policies in AWS are applied to STS sessions and can only restrict (not expand) permissions — the effective permissions are the intersection of the session policy and the IAM entity's identity-based policies. Even though the user's inline policy allows both iam:CreateAccessKey and iam:CreateUser, the session policy restricts available actions to only iam:CreateAccessKey. The session policy cannot grant permissions beyond what the user's identity-based policies allow.

q: External identity provider (Okta) SAML assertion specifies IAM role to assume. Compromised assertion could specify unauthorized role. Mitigation?
options: SAML inherently secure (not true)
Validate SAML Principal matches expected roles in trust policy, use condition keys to restrict assumed roles
Disable SAML federation (breaks integration)
AWS always validates SAML (assumption)
explain: SAML federation allows the IdP to specify which IAM role to assume in the SAML assertion, and a compromised or manipulated assertion could specify an unauthorized high-privilege role. The trust policy must validate the SAML Principal attribute matches expected IdP-issued role ARNs and use condition keys (saml:aud, saml:iss) to verify the assertion was issued by the correct IdP for the correct audience. AWS does not automatically validate SAML assertion integrity beyond signature verification.

q: Role chaining: Role A assumes Role B for multi-step API operations. Security consideration?
options: Session duration unlimited by default
Chaining audit logging unnecessary
Chaining unrestricted and unlimited
Limit chaining depth + session duration, verify Role B trust only allows Role A
explain: Role chaining (Role A assumes Role B) is a legitimate pattern for multi-step workflows, but it can enable privilege escalation if Role B's trust policy is not restricted to only allow Role A. The maximum session duration for chained roles is limited to 1 hour regardless of the MaxSessionDuration setting on either role. Trust policies must explicitly verify that only the intended role (Role A) can chain to Role B to prevent unauthorized privilege escalation.

q: IAM policy allows 's3:*' except 'restricted-bucket'. Explicit deny policy denies 's3:*' all buckets. User can do what?
options: Access all except 'restricted-bucket'
Nothing; explicit deny on all buckets overrides any allow
Access only 'restricted-bucket'
Depends on policy order
explain: Explicit deny policies in IAM apply universally and cannot be overridden by allow policies at any level. A policy with an explicit deny on s3:* for all resources means no S3 action is permitted, regardless of what other allow policies grant. Even the allow policy excepting restricted-bucket cannot override the explicit deny because deny evaluation precedes allow evaluation in IAM policy processing.

q: Auditor assumes cross-account role (1-hour session). After 59 minutes, attempts assume again. What happens?
options: Receives new session with fresh credentials; previous session expires on its own schedule
Duration doesn't affect re-assumption
Can't assume within 1 hour
Previous session extends indefinitely
explain: Each AssumeRole call generates an independent set of temporary credentials (access key, secret key, session token) with its own expiration timestamp. Assuming the same role again before the current session expires creates a new, separate session with a fresh expiration — it does not extend or replace the existing session. Both sessions remain valid until their individual expiration times.

q: Organization SCP denies CloudTrail disable/delete. Admin developers can't test IAM policies affecting CloudTrail. Balance by?
options: Create development account exempt from SCP; dev OUs different policies than production
Disable SCP entirely (loses protection)
Developers can't test with SCPs (not true)
Give root account exceptions (anti-pattern)
explain: SCPs are attached at the AWS Organizations OU level, enabling different policy sets for different account types. Attaching the CloudTrail protection SCP (denying cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail) to production OUs while excluding it from development OUs allows production accounts to maintain immutable audit trails while developers retain flexibility to test CloudTrail configurations. The management account is exempt from SCPs by default, so it always retains CloudTrail management capability.

q: Organization denies all iam:PassRole for non-service principals. Developers can't create EC2 instance profiles. Resolve by?
options: Remove all iam:PassRole restrictions (security risk)
Developers can't create profiles with SCP
Use S3 IAM roles instead
Create IAM role with iam:PassRole limited to specific profiles, or SCP conditions allowing iam:PassRole for specific roles only
explain: The iam:PassRole permission controls which IAM roles can be assigned to AWS services (such as EC2 instance profiles or Lambda execution roles). An SCP that denies all iam:PassRole prevents developers from creating EC2 instance profiles. The correct solution is to create an IAM policy or SCP condition that allows iam:PassRole only for specific, pre-approved role ARNs, implementing the principle of least privilege for role assignment without blocking all instance profile creation.

q: STS temporary credentials (1-hour duration). Application needs 8-hour session for batch job. Solve by?
options: Increase DurationSeconds beyond maximum (not allowed)
Batch job can't use STS
Use STS with longer DurationSeconds (max 12 hours) or implement app refresh logic before session expires
Store permanent IAM credentials in app (insecure)
explain: AWS STS AssumeRole has a configurable DurationSeconds parameter with a maximum of 43,200 seconds (12 hours) for role assumption calls (longer for IAM users assuming roles with no configured maximum). For an 8-hour batch job, setting DurationSeconds to 28,800 seconds provides the required duration within the STS limits. Applications with extremely long-running sessions should implement token refresh logic that re-assumes the role before the session expires, ensuring uninterrupted execution.

q: Role assumption requires IP from corporate VPN. However, VPN masks actual user IP. Effective?
options: No, VPN masks actual IP; use MFA requirement or identity provider device posture checks instead
No way to authenticate corporate users remotely
VPN IP condition works (not true)
This is intended IP condition use
explain: IP-based role assumption conditions (aws:SourceIp) are ineffective for VPN-based access because the VPN server's IP is what AWS sees as the source address, not the individual user's machine IP. All VPN users would share the same source IP, making IP conditions unable to distinguish between authorized and unauthorized users. MFA conditions (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent) or identity provider-enforced device posture checks provide individual-level authentication that IP conditions cannot.

q: Developer on laptop without corporate VPN or MFA attempts to assume a cross-account IAM role for production resource access. How should you secure this remote access scenario?
options: MFA alone is sufficient for remote access without additional context
Verified Access is only for applications, not IAM role assumption
Laptop access is secure without additional controls (not true)
Require MFA device in assume role trust policy (mfa_authenticated:true), enforce corporate VPN or use Verified Access for zero-trust device verification
explain: For remote developer access to production AWS resources, combining MFA requirement in the role's trust policy (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: true) with AWS Verified Access for device compliance verification provides defense-in-depth: MFA ensures the user authenticated with a second factor, and Verified Access checks device posture (patch level, endpoint detection, certificate) before granting access. Relying on MFA alone cannot verify the device is not compromised.

q: IAM policy includes wildcard resource '*' but with specific actions (s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject). Too permissive?
options: Wildcards fine with restricted actions
Yes, restrict to specific bucket ARNs like arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*
Specific actions override resource wildcards
Action restrictions make wildcard safe
explain: IAM policies using wildcard resources (Resource: *) for data-plane actions (s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject) grant access to all S3 buckets and objects in the account and all accounts accessible through resource-based policies, creating an unnecessarily broad permission scope. The principle of least privilege requires scoping resources to specific bucket ARNs and prefixes (arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*). Specific action restrictions with wildcard resources still expose all resources of that type.

q: Contractor from external company on laptop attempts to assume temporary credentials for accessing AWS resources remotely without VPN. Security approach?
options: Laptop access is inherently insecure (not entirely true with controls)
Require MFA in trust policy, use Verified Access for device compliance verification before allowing role assumption
Verified Access cannot be used for contractor access
MFA alone is sufficient without device posture checks
explain: For contractor remote access without a corporate VPN, requiring MFA in the IAM role trust policy ensures the contractor authenticated with a second factor before assuming the role. AWS Verified Access adds device compliance verification, checking that the contractor's device meets security standards (OS patch level, EDR software) before granting access. This combination provides strong authentication and device-level access control without requiring VPN infrastructure.

q: DynamoDB encrypted with AWS-managed key. Migration to customer-managed KMS needed. Approach?
options: Use S3 to copy DynamoDB (unsupported)
Delete old table, recreate with new key (data loss)
Create new table with customer-managed key, replicate data via DMS/script, verify before app cutover
Assume AWS-managed keys sufficient
explain: DynamoDB does not support in-place encryption key migration — the encryption key is specified at table creation time and cannot be changed on an existing table. Migrating to a customer-managed KMS key requires creating a new table with the CMK encryption setting, replicating data using AWS Database Migration Service or a custom script, verifying data integrity, then cutting over the application. AWS Data Migration Service can replicate DynamoDB data with minimal downtime during migration.

q: KMS key policy: security team manages key. Operations needs encrypt/decrypt without key management. Grant access by?
options: Assume users can't have specific KMS perms
Store KMS keys in app code
Create KMS grant (kms:Encrypt, kms:Decrypt) with EncryptionContextSubset constraint
Give everyone root account access (insecure)
explain: AWS KMS grants provide delegated, temporary permissions for specific KMS operations without modifying the key's permanent resource policy. An Operations team grant for kms:Encrypt and kms:Decrypt with an EncryptionContextSubset constraint limits usage to specific data contexts, providing least-privilege access. Unlike key policy statements (which are permanent), grants can be retired programmatically when the access is no longer needed, making them ideal for time-limited cross-team key usage.

q: Application needs auto-rotating database credentials (30-day rotation). Credentials stored insecurely. Best approach?
options: Hardcode in environment variables
Manual 30-day rotation reliable
AWS Secrets Manager with Lambda rotation function updating password, auto-returning new creds
Plaintext files
explain: AWS Secrets Manager provides automatic credential rotation by invoking a Lambda rotation function that generates a new password, updates the database, tests the new credentials, and stores the new version — all without application downtime. The application retrieves credentials from Secrets Manager on each use, always getting the current active version. Hardcoding credentials in environment variables or plaintext files creates long-term exposure risk and requires manual rotation.

q: Compliance requires organization's private CA, not public internet CA. Implement by?
options: Assume ACM public certs meet compliance
Manually create and manage all certs
Use ACM Private CA to create internal CA, issue and distribute certificates
Self-sign without CA
explain: AWS Certificate Manager Private CA allows organizations to create a fully managed private Certificate Authority hierarchy for issuing internal TLS certificates that are not trusted by public browsers or certificate stores. This satisfies compliance requirements for internal CA-issued certificates while eliminating the operational burden of managing CA infrastructure. Self-signed certificates cannot be validated against a trusted CA chain, and ACM public certificates are issued by a public CA rather than the organization's own CA.

q: S3 bucket: immutable data requirement. Enable Object Lock compliance mode. Root attempts delete before retention expires?
options: Root can always delete
Deletion denied even for root; compliance mode irreversible and cannot override
Deletion allowed if retention shortens
Object Lock only prevents accidents
explain: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode creates WORM (Write Once, Read Many) protection where no principal — including the account root user or AWS Support — can delete or overwrite a locked object until the retention period expires. This is the AWS mechanism for satisfying strict data immutability requirements such as SEC Rule 17a-4 or HIPAA audit log requirements. Governance mode (which this question is NOT about) can be overridden by users with the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission.

q: RDS Oracle database instance stores sensitive financial data at rest. RDS encryption via customer-managed KMS key is configured. However, Oracle TDE is not yet enabled. Are both encryption layers necessary for compliance?
options: RDS encryption is sufficient; Oracle TDE is redundant
TDE alone without RDS encryption provides adequate protection
Both layers are equivalent; only one should be implemented
Yes, both are necessary: RDS encryption protects the storage volume layer, Oracle TDE provides additional database-layer encryption for defense-in-depth
explain: Both RDS-level encryption (which encrypts the EBS storage volume where RDS data is stored) and Oracle Transparent Data Encryption (which encrypts data within the database engine before writing to storage) provide valuable but distinct protections. RDS encryption protects data at rest in the underlying storage infrastructure; Oracle TDE provides application-level encryption that protects against threats from within the database layer itself. Defense-in-depth through dual encryption layers meets the stricter requirements of financial compliance frameworks.

q: KMS key rotation: 1 year. Compliance requires audit trail of all key operations. Configure by?
options: Disable rotation to reduce API calls
Assume KMS operations auto-logged
Use S3 for key rotation logs
Enable CloudTrail logging for KMS API calls, set automatic rotation to 1 year
explain: AWS KMS automatic key rotation (enabled on Customer Managed Keys) rotates the underlying cryptographic key material annually and logs the rotation event in CloudTrail (via the RotateKey API call), providing a complete audit trail for compliance purposes. CloudTrail KMS events include the key ARN, caller identity, and timestamp, satisfying auditor requirements for key operation logging. Disabling rotation to reduce API calls removes a critical security control.

q: Secrets Manager secret replicated to 3 regions for DR. Attacker deletes in one region. Impact on replicas?
options: No impact, deletion triggers auto-recovery from replicas; cross-region replication provides HA
Replicas read-only after deletion
Yes, deletion removes all copies
Deletion only affects primary region
explain: Secrets Manager cross-region replication creates read replicas of secrets in secondary regions, providing high availability for applications that need to retrieve credentials in multiple regions. When the primary secret is deleted (or the region experiences an outage), applications in secondary regions can continue retrieving credentials from their regional replica. However, once the primary is deleted, replicas eventually become orphaned and may need to be promoted to primary for normal operation.

q: RDS MySQL: auto-backups encrypted via KMS. KMS key deleted. Restore from backups?
options: Unencrypted data restorable
No, backups encrypted with deleted key can't be decrypted; use key policy MFA delete or approval to prevent deletion
Backup encryption separate from key management
Yes, backups unaffected by key deletion
explain: RDS automated backups are encrypted using the same KMS key as the database instance. If that KMS key is deleted (or the key material is permanently destroyed), the backup ciphertext cannot be decrypted and the backups are permanently inaccessible. KMS keys should be protected with key policies requiring MFA for deletion and deletion approval workflows (minimum 7-day deletion window). Restoring from backups after key deletion is impossible — the backup ciphertext is permanently locked.

q: Macie reports unencrypted S3 objects. Enable default S3 encryption with KMS. Existing unencrypted objects?
options: Existing objects auto-encrypt
Default applies only to new objects; use S3 Batch Operations or CLI to re-encrypt existing objects
Delete unencrypted (data loss)
Manually re-encrypt each object
explain: S3 default encryption (bucket-level encryption setting) applies to all new objects uploaded to the bucket after the setting is enabled, but does not retroactively encrypt objects that existed before the setting was applied. Existing unencrypted objects require S3 Batch Operations (using S3:CopyObject) to read each object and write it back with the KMS encryption header applied, or the AWS CLI with a script that performs the same copy-to-encrypt operation. Macie identifies the unencrypted objects but cannot perform the encryption itself.

q: S3 encryption with KMS context (Department:Finance). App reads encrypted files. Implement access control via context by?
options: Store context plaintext with data
Context only for metadata, not access control
S3 bucket policy without context
KMS grants with EncryptionContextSubset; include context during PutObject and require matching in GetObject
explain: KMS encryption context is authenticated additional data that is included in the encryption operation and must match exactly during decryption. By including a context key-value pair (e.g., Department: Finance) during PutObject encryption and requiring the same context during GetObject decryption via a KMS grant with EncryptionContextSubset, applications can enforce fine-grained access control at the encryption layer. An application without the correct encryption context will receive an InvalidCiphertextException when attempting to decrypt.

q: AWS CloudHSM generates/stores encryption keys. Unlike KMS, CloudHSM requires customer-managed backups. DR strategy?
options: Store CloudHSM keys in S3
CloudHSM handles DR automatically (not true)
Single HSM instance accept key loss risk
Multiple HSM instances across AZs, enable key backup to secure external storage
explain: AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated, single-tenant hardware security module instances where the customer fully controls the HSM and AWS has no access to key material or cryptographic operations. Because CloudHSM is customer-managed hardware (dedicated to a single customer), customer-managed backup procedures are required — unlike KMS where AWS manages backup and availability automatically. For DR, CloudHSM clusters should span multiple AZs with at least 2 HSM instances, and keys should be exported to an encrypted backup with secure external storage.

q: PrivateLink endpoint to internal Certificate Authority issues TLS certs. Only authorized apps should request. Authentication?
options: IP-based filtering only
Accept all requests
Trust all AWS resources (incorrect)
Require mTLS with app certificates, validate against whitelist
explain: Mutual TLS (mTLS) for a PrivateLink endpoint to an internal CA provides bi-directional authentication: the CA server verifies the client's certificate (confirming it is an authorized application) and the client verifies the server's certificate. Only applications with pre-issued, whitelisted client certificates can request new certificates from the CA. IP-based filtering alone cannot authenticate application identity and can be spoofed within a VPC.

q: DynamoDB Global Tables encrypted with customer-managed KMS key. Cross-region replication requires what KMS config?
options: Use different keys per region (breaks global tables)
Cross-region encryption auto-configured (not true)
Key policy allows DynamoDB service principal to use key for encrypt/decrypt in all target regions
Disable encryption for replication (security risk)
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables with customer-managed KMS keys require the KMS key policy to explicitly grant the DynamoDB service principal permission to use the key (kms:Decrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, kms:DescribeKey) in each region where a replica table exists. Cross-region replication for Global Tables uses the same KMS key to encrypt replicated data, so the key policy must permit DynamoDB to use the key for replication operations across all target regions. Using different keys per region would break the Global Tables replication mechanism.

q: Application encrypts data with KMS symmetric key. Data file shared externally, key not shared. Compromised?
options: No, key access control protects data; implement key audit and consider rotation after sharing
Yes, data exposed after sharing
External parties always decrypt AWS data
Symmetric encryption unsafe after sharing
explain: Encrypting data with a KMS symmetric key and then sharing only the ciphertext (not the key) provides strong security because without KMS decrypt permissions, the recipient cannot access the plaintext regardless of how many copies of the ciphertext exist. Key access control (KMS key policy) determines who can decrypt, not who has the ciphertext. KMS CloudTrail audit logging records every decrypt attempt, and key rotation after sharing ensures the key material for previously encrypted data cannot be accessed with a new key version.

q: Mobile app cert pinning prevents MITM. ACM cert rotates unexpectedly. Impact and mitigation?
options: Pinning auto-updates (not true)
Pinning prevents rotation
Rotation has no impact
App can't connect; monitor Certificate Transparency logs, implement dynamic pinning with backup certs
explain: Certificate pinning hardcodes a certificate's public key or fingerprint in the application, causing TLS connections to fail when ACM rotates the certificate and changes the public key. The mitigation is to implement dynamic pinning by pinning to the intermediate CA certificate (which changes less frequently than leaf certificates) and monitoring Certificate Transparency logs for certificate issuance events that signal upcoming rotation. Backup certificate pins can be pre-loaded before rotation to prevent application outages.

q: S3 bucket requires Object Lock LEGAL_HOLD + retention. Legal hold prevents what?
options: Can't coexist with retention
Even with expired retention, legal hold prevents deletion until admin explicitly removes it
Applies only to new objects
Same as retention period
explain: S3 Object Lock legal hold is independent from retention period configuration — an object under legal hold cannot be deleted or overwritten until an authorized user explicitly removes the hold, even if the object's retention period has already expired. This is designed for litigation holds where an indeterminate hold duration is required. Legal holds and retention periods can coexist on the same object; both must be removed before deletion is possible.

q: RDS encrypted backup restored to dev. Dev DBA has decrypt access. After test, protect original data by?
options: Delete backup after restore
Leave decrypt access unchanged (security risk)
KMS key policy restricts decrypt access to production DBAs in production accounts, revoke dev access
Assume decrypted backup contains no sensitive data
explain: When an RDS encrypted backup is restored to a development environment, the KMS key used for the production backup grants the development DBA access to production encryption keys. After testing, the KMS key policy should be updated to remove the development DBA's decrypt permissions (restricting kms:Decrypt to production account production DBA principals only). Simply deleting the backup after restoration does not revoke the key access that was established during the restore.

q: Your organization generates encryption key material offline in an air-gapped system using NIST-approved cryptographic procedures. How do you safely import this key material into AWS KMS?
options: Import keys via plaintext transmission (extremely insecure)
Key material import process does not require special security considerations
KMS auto-generates keys; offline import is not recommended for any use case
Use KMS import token and encrypted key material transport mechanism; ensure KMS key policy restricts who can import keys
explain: KMS key material import requires using an import token obtained from KMS and encrypting the external key material using KMS's RSA wrapping public key before transmission. The encrypted key material is then submitted to KMS via the ImportKeyMaterial API call. The KMS key policy must restrict kms:ImportKeyMaterial to only authorized administrators who should have this privileged operation. Transmitting key material in plaintext would expose it to interception.

q: Secrets Manager API key with 90-day rotation. Rotation Lambda fails silently (returns success, doesn't update). Detect by?
options: Rotation failures are obvious (not always)
Assume rotation succeeds if no error
Manual verification only
Enable CloudTrail logging, check version history in console, CloudWatch alarm on rotation events
explain: Secrets Manager rotation monitoring requires proactive validation because a rotation Lambda can return a success status without actually updating the secret value. CloudTrail logs all Secrets Manager API calls including RotateSecret and the Lambda invocation, and the secret's version history in the console shows whether a new AWSPENDING version was successfully promoted to AWSCURRENT. A CloudWatch alarm on the rotation Lambda's error metrics can alert on silent failures.

q: Your organization encrypts customer payment card data in RDS Oracle database using both RDS KMS encryption and Oracle Transparent Data Encryption (TDE). Why implement both layers?
options: Implementing both layers creates conflicts and performance degradation
Oracle TDE makes RDS encryption redundant and unnecessary
RDS encryption alone is completely sufficient for PCI DSS compliance
Defense-in-depth: RDS encryption protects the encrypted storage volume; Oracle TDE encrypts data within the database, protecting against insider threats and providing application-level security
explain: Defense-in-depth encryption with both RDS KMS (storage-layer encryption) and Oracle TDE (database-layer encryption) provides protection at two distinct attack surfaces: RDS encryption protects against physical media theft or unauthorized storage access, while Oracle TDE protects against threats that bypass the storage layer and access data through the database engine (such as insider threats or application-layer attacks). Organizations subject to stringent compliance frameworks like PCI DSS often require this dual-layer approach.

q: Your organization manages cryptographic key material that was generated offline in a secure facility and must be imported into AWS KMS for use by database encryption operations. What is the secure import process?
options: Use KMS key import workflow: obtain import token, wrap key with public key, transmit encrypted material, verify import via CloudTrail; restrict import permissions in KMS key policy
Email key material to AWS support for manual import (violates security)
KMS does not support importing offline-generated key material
Import key material without any encryption or access controls (insecure)
explain: The secure process for importing externally generated key material into KMS: (1) Call GetParametersForImport to obtain an import token and KMS's RSA wrapping public key, (2) Encrypt the key material using the wrapping public key with RSAES-OAEP-SHA-256, (3) Call ImportKeyMaterial with the encrypted key material and import token, (4) Verify the import succeeded via CloudTrail. The key policy must restrict kms:ImportKeyMaterial to authorized administrators only.

q: RDS PostgreSQL database stores highly sensitive healthcare data (PHI under HIPAA) encrypted with AWS KMS at the storage layer. Oracle database in separate account uses Oracle TDE at the database layer. Compare encryption strategies.
options: Only Oracle TDE meets HIPAA requirements; KMS is insufficient
Neither encryption method meets healthcare compliance requirements
Only RDS KMS encryption meets HIPAA requirements
Both are valid: PostgreSQL with KMS provides storage-layer encryption, Oracle with TDE provides database-layer encryption; both meet HIPAA encryption requirements when properly configured
explain: Both RDS KMS encryption (for PostgreSQL storage encryption) and Oracle TDE (for Oracle database-layer encryption) satisfy HIPAA encryption requirements when properly configured, as HIPAA specifies encryption standards but not specific technology implementations. The choice between them depends on the database engine, compliance framework, and defense-in-depth requirements. Using both where applicable provides stronger protection than either alone.

q: Organization member accounts shouldn't disable CloudTrail. SCP or resource-based policy for prevention?
options: Assume developers won't disable
Disable CloudTrail to prevent config
Use SCP to deny cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail in member accounts
Resource-based policies alone insufficient
explain: SCPs with the deny action for cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail at the organization level prevent any IAM principal in member accounts — including account administrators — from disabling or deleting CloudTrail. Resource-based policies on the CloudTrail S3 bucket can restrict writes, but they cannot prevent the StopLogging API call itself. SCPs are the only preventive control that operates above the IAM layer.

q: Control Tower guardrail: public S3 buckets detected in 2 accounts. Preventive or detective guardrail?
options: All guardrails preventive
Bucket creation was prevented
Guardrails only for new accounts
Detective guardrail detects violations after occurring
explain: AWS Control Tower uses detective guardrails to identify policy violations after they occur, reporting them through AWS Config. A public S3 bucket detected by a detective guardrail means the bucket was created after the guardrail was applied — the guardrail detected the violation but did not prevent it. Preventive guardrails use SCPs to block the violating action before it occurs. Both types serve different purposes in a multi-layer governance strategy.

q: AWS Config aggregator from 5 member accounts. Attacker disables Config in one. Aggregator detects change?
options: Config can't be disabled by non-root
No, aggregator blind after Config disabled
Aggregator auto re-enables Config
Yes, Config changes recorded in aggregator and CloudTrail logs who disabled
explain: AWS Config aggregators collect configuration and compliance data from member accounts using organization-level authorization. If an attacker disables Config in a member account, the aggregator will stop receiving updated data from that account, but the last-known configuration state remains in the aggregator. The disabling action itself is recorded in CloudTrail (DeleteConfigurationRecorder or StopConfigurationRecorder API call) with the IAM identity of the actor. The aggregator cannot automatically re-enable Config in a member account.

q: Control Tower Account Factory provisions development account. Placement in organization hierarchy?
options: Account Factory can't automate creation
Designated OU from Account Factory config (typically 'Workloads' OU) with guardrails applied
Organization root
Developer chooses OU
explain: AWS Control Tower Account Factory creates new accounts and places them in a pre-configured Organizational Unit (typically 'Workloads/Dev', 'Workloads/Prod', or a custom OU specified in the Account Factory configuration). GuardDuty, Security Hub, and Config are automatically enabled in the new account through their AWS Organizations integrations. The developer does not choose the OU placement; Account Factory enforces the governance structure.

q: Security Hub + Slack/PagerDuty/Jira. GuardDuty critical finding should create Jira ticket + Slack alert. How?
options: Security Hub can't integrate with third-party
Manually monitor and create tickets
EventBridge routes Security Hub findings to Lambda, Lambda creates Jira ticket and posts Slack webhook
Email notifications only
explain: Security Hub acts as the central aggregator and EventBridge serves as the integration layer for downstream systems. An EventBridge rule matching GuardDuty findings forwarded to Security Hub can trigger a Lambda function that invokes the Jira REST API to create a ticket and posts to a Slack webhook for immediate notification. Security Hub does not natively integrate with third-party tools — EventBridge and Lambda provide the bridge.

q: Organization 100+ accounts. Attacker creates public EC2 instance. Prevent across all accounts by?
options: SCP denying ec2:RunInstances with AssociatePublicIpAddress=true in all OUs
Monitor CloudTrail and delete after (reactive)
Disable EC2 in member accounts
Assume developers won't create public
explain: An SCP with a condition denying ec2:RunInstances when ec2:AssociatePublicIpAddress=true prevents all instances from being launched with a public IP address, effectively enforcing private subnet deployment across all member accounts. This is a preventive control that blocks the action before it occurs. CloudTrail monitoring and remediation after the fact allows public instances to exist briefly before detection and remediation.

q: AWS Firewall Manager centrally manages WAF for 50 accounts. New threat needs WAF rule update. Deploy by?
options: Manually update each account's WAF
WAF rules don't need org-wide consistency
Store rules in S3, distribute manually
Create WebACL policy in Firewall Manager; auto-deploys to all specified accounts
explain: AWS Firewall Manager with a Security Hub WebACL policy centrally manages WAF rules across all accounts in an organization. When a new threat requires a WAF rule update, adding the rule to the Firewall Manager policy automatically deploys it to all specified accounts' WebACLs without requiring manual access to each account. This eliminates the operational burden of managing WAF rules independently in 50 accounts.

q: Incident response: EC2 instance possibly compromised. Termination not option. Preserve forensic evidence (memory, logs). Actions?
options: Create AMI before isolation (preserves malware)
Leave running (attackers continue)
Immediately terminate
Stop instance (preserve volume), extract logs via Session Manager, optionally capture memory, enable CloudTrail for instance activity
explain: For a potentially compromised EC2 instance that cannot be terminated, the forensically sound approach is to stop the instance (which preserves EBS volumes in their current state), use Session Manager to extract application and OS logs before stopping, optionally capture volatile memory before stopping using a memory capture tool, and ensure CloudTrail logging for all instance-related API activity is preserved. Creating an AMI before isolation is useful but AMIs do not preserve live memory state. Leaving the instance running allows the attacker to continue operating.

q: Incident response: CloudTrail logs needed 7 years. Budget requires cheaper storage after 30 days. Implementation?
options: Keep all Standard indefinitely
Delete after 30 days (compliance risk)
CloudTrail auto-retains
S3 Lifecycle (Standard→Glacier→Deep Archive by timespan), S3 Object Lock compliance mode 7yr, log file validation
explain: CloudTrail logs must be retained for 7 years per the compliance requirement, but constant access to 7-year-old logs is not needed. An S3 Lifecycle policy automating transitions from Standard to Glacier to Deep Archive reduces storage costs significantly while maintaining compliance. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode ensures logs cannot be deleted or modified before the retention period expires, satisfying the tamper-proof requirement. CloudTrail does not manage its own long-term retention.

q: Control Tower: Non-compliant guardrail in 10 accounts. Detective guardrails need remediation automation. How?
options: All guardrails auto-remediate
EventBridge detects Config findings, triggers Lambda remediation; detective guardrails don't auto-remediate
Manual remediation only
Control Tower has no remediation
explain: AWS Control Tower detective guardrails (implemented as AWS Config rules) identify policy violations but do not perform automatic remediation — they only report non-compliance. To automate remediation of detective guardrail violations, an EventBridge rule must detect Config rule non-compliance events and trigger a Lambda function that performs the corrective action (e.g., removing a public S3 bucket policy, enabling encryption). This pattern provides automated remediation while maintaining detective guardrail visibility.

q: CloudFormation templates across 50+ accounts. Security review: overpermissive IAM policies. Prevent future?
options: Use CloudFormation Guard to validate templates against security rules, integrate with CI/CD to block non-compliant
CloudFormation has no validation
Manually review all templates
Use SCPs to block CloudFormation
explain: CloudFormation Guard is a policy-as-code framework that evaluates CloudFormation templates against security rules before deployment. Integrating Guard checks into CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, CodePipeline) prevents non-compliant templates from reaching production by failing the pipeline when templates contain overpermissive IAM policies, open security groups, or disabled encryption. Retrospective SCPs and manual reviews do not prevent insecure templates from being deployed.

q: Organization root credentials compromised. Immediate damage-limiting before rotation?
options: Root has unlimited unrestricted perms (not true, SCPs apply)
Use SCPs to restrict root actions (SCPs apply to root), deny high-risk actions, enable MFA monitoring
Accept unlimited access until rotation
Delete root account (impossible)
explain: When an organization root account is compromised, SCPs can be applied from the management account to restrict high-risk actions (such as creating IAM users, disabling CloudTrail, or creating root access keys) in all member accounts — limiting the blast radius of the compromise. However, the management account itself is exempt from its own SCPs, so the root user of the management account cannot be restricted via SCP. Enabling MFA monitoring and rotating root credentials remains essential.

q: Centralized logging: 100 accounts send CloudTrail to security account S3. Prevent unauthorized access by?
options: VPC endpoints only, no policies
Assume CloudTrail logs always private
S3 bucket policy allows only CloudTrail service principal and security account principals
Make bucket public
explain: A central S3 logging bucket receiving CloudTrail from 100 member accounts requires a bucket policy with an explicit Allow for the CloudTrail service principal (delivery.logs.amazonaws.com) with an aws:SourceOrgID or aws:SourceAccount condition scoping the grant to the organization's member accounts. Without this policy, cross-account CloudTrail log delivery is blocked by S3's default deny behavior. Making the bucket public exposes audit logs to unauthorized parties.

q: Your organization has centralized AWS Backup for all 100+ member accounts managed through an organization root. One member account is not creating any automated backups despite having backup plans configured. What could cause this?
options: AWS Backup does not support EC2 instances for backup (unsupported claim)
All member accounts automatically inherit backup capabilities without configuration
AWS Backup only works in the organization primary account, not member accounts
The backup service role may lack permissions in that account, or the member account hasn't authorized the Backup service; verify IAM role permissions and cross-account backup authorization
explain: AWS Backup with centralized management through AWS Organizations requires each member account's backup service role to have sufficient IAM permissions to create and manage backups for the resources in that account. Additionally, cross-account backup requires the member account to authorize AWS Backup to operate using the service-linked role. If either the IAM role permissions or the cross-account backup authorization is missing, AWS Backup cannot create automated backups in that account.

q: AWS Config Rules: non-compliant instances detected. Auto-remediation Lambda doesn't trigger. Check what?
options: Config rule has auto-remediation enabled, SSM document exists with permissions, check CloudTrail for attempts and errors
Config Rules don't support remediation
Disable rule, fix manually
Assume Lambda running without verification
explain: AWS Config Rule auto-remediation requires three correctly configured components: (1) the rule must have auto-remediation enabled with the correct SSM document, (2) the SSM document must exist and be properly configured, and (3) the IAM role used by the remediation must have permissions to execute the SSM document and perform the remediation actions. CloudTrail logs the attempted SSM execution and any IAM access denied errors, providing the debugging trail.

q: AWS Organizations: member accounts should not stop RDS instances during production hours. Prevent via?
options: Assume developers won't stop instances
SCP denying rds:StopDBInstance with DateGreaterThan condition for business hours in all OUs
Resource-based policies alone insufficient
Disable RDS in member accounts
explain: An SCP can include time-based conditions using aws:CurrentTime or aws:RequestedRegion conditions to restrict rds:StopDBInstance during business hours, preventing member account administrators from stopping production RDS instances during production windows. This preventive control operates at the organization level without requiring per-account configuration. Resource-based policies cannot prevent IAM user-initiated API calls because resource policies only control access to the resource itself, not the IAM actions.

q: Control Tower: preventive guardrail blocks EC2 creation in dev account. Developer needs instance for testing. Resolution?
options: Request SCP exception for dev OU, or create temporary override for guardrail
Disable guardrail entirely (security risk)
Use non-AWS services instead
Assume guardrail can't be overridden (not true)
explain: AWS Control Tower allows OU-level guardrail exceptions by moving development accounts to a separate OU with different guardrail configurations, or by creating a customized SCP exception for the specific test scenario. Disabling the guardrail entirely for all accounts removes the protection for production accounts. The correct governance pattern is to enforce stricter guardrails in production OUs and allow necessary flexibility in development OUs.

q: Security Hub: 500+ daily findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, IAM Access Analyzer. Team can't keep up. Prioritize by?
options: Custom insights: high severity + remediation time < 1 day, route to incident response with PagerDuty integration
Disable less critical services (lose visibility)
Manually review all findings
Archive findings without review
explain: Security Hub custom insights with filter combinations (severity=HIGH+CRITICAL, workflow_status=NEW, resource_type=[EC2, S3], finding_provider=GuardDuty) create prioritized finding views that match the security team's operational workflow. Integrating these insights with PagerDuty via EventBridge automation routes only the highest-priority findings for on-call response. Disabling data sources removes security visibility, while archiving findings without review creates audit gaps.

q: GuardDuty finding: UnprotectedS3Bucket indicates an S3 bucket allows public GetObject. How do you remediate?
options: Delete the bucket
Make it more public
Apply bucket policy to deny GetObject from anonymous principals, enable Block Public Access, review S3 access logs to see who accessed the bucket
Can't fix public buckets
explain: GuardDuty S3 Protection detects suspicious access to S3 buckets by monitoring S3 data events for anomalous patterns. An UnprotectedS3Bucket-related finding indicates a bucket configuration issue. The remediation requires applying an explicit deny policy for s3:GetObject to anonymous (unauthenticated) principals, enabling S3 Block Public Access, and reviewing S3 server access logs to determine what data was accessed during the period of public exposure. Deleting the bucket destroys business data.

q: Detective investigation shows a Lambda function was invoked 1000 times in 5 minutes (unusual spike), and each invocation accessed a sensitive S3 bucket. What does this suggest?
options: Dismiss spike findings as expected noise from normal CloudFront traffic fluctuations
Always a false positive
Either legitimate load spike (check business context), or Lambda function compromised (query CloudTrail for what data was accessed, throttle function, review code)
Lambda functions can't be compromised
explain: Amazon Detective's entity profiles automatically ingest GuardDuty findings, CloudTrail logs, and VPC Flow Logs to build behavioral baselines and highlight deviations. A Lambda invocation spike to a sensitive S3 bucket needs business context: a known batch job or scheduled event would explain legitimate volume, while the same spike without a known trigger warrants CloudTrail investigation to determine what data was accessed and whether credentials were exfiltrated. Context distinguishes false positives from real incidents.

q: A security engineer receives an Amazon GuardDuty finding of type 'UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/MaliciousIPCaller.Custom' for an IAM user in a production account. The finding indicates API calls were made from an IP address on the company's threat list. What should the security engineer do FIRST?
options: Add the IP address to a Network ACL deny rule to block future access, then review VPC Flow Logs for the traffic from that IP
Disable the compromised IAM user's access keys immediately using IAM, then investigate CloudTrail logs to determine what actions were taken from the malicious IP
Create a GuardDuty suppression rule for the finding type to reduce noise while the investigation proceeds
Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the account to protect against further attacks from the malicious IP address
explain: When a GuardDuty finding shows API calls from a custom threat list IP for a specific IAM user, the immediate response is containment: disable the access keys (iam:UpdateAccessKey with Status=Inactive) to stop any ongoing unauthorized access, then investigate CloudTrail for what API calls were made from the malicious IP. Blocking the IP via NACL addresses only that source IP and doesn't prevent the credentials from being used from other locations. AWS Shield protects against DDoS, not credential misuse.

q: A company wants to detect when Amazon S3 objects containing sensitive data are made publicly accessible. The security team requires near-real-time detection (within 5 minutes) and automatic remediation. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Configure AWS Config with the 's3-bucket-public-read-prohibited' managed rule and enable automatic remediation using the AWS-DisableS3BucketPublicReadWrite SSM Automation document
Enable Amazon Macie to monitor S3 buckets for policy changes, create an Amazon EventBridge rule to trigger a Lambda function that removes public access when Macie generates a finding
Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for S3, create an EventBridge rule matching 'PutBucketPolicy' and 'PutBucketAcl' events, and trigger a Lambda function to revert public access
Enable S3 server access logging on all buckets, create a CloudWatch Metric Filter for 'PutBucketAcl' events, and trigger an SNS alert when detected
explain: CloudTrail data events for S3 capture every PutBucketPolicy and PutBucketAcl API call in near-real-time. An EventBridge rule matching these events triggers a Lambda function within seconds to revert any public access configuration — meeting the 5-minute requirement. Amazon Macie performs content-based data classification and can detect bucket policy changes, but finding generation can take longer than 5 minutes. S3 server access logs have delivery delays making them unsuitable for near-real-time remediation.

q: A security team discovers that an Amazon EC2 instance in a production VPC is communicating with an external IP address that GuardDuty has flagged as a known command-and-control server. The instance runs a critical application that cannot be taken offline immediately. What should the security team do to contain the threat while maintaining application availability?
options: Create a VPC Network ACL deny rule for outbound traffic to the command-and-control IP address and associate it with the instance's subnet
Enable Amazon Inspector on the instance to scan for vulnerabilities and install the patch automatically to stop the malicious communication
Take a snapshot of the instance's EBS volumes for forensic analysis and terminate the instance, then restore from the last clean snapshot
Modify the security group attached to the instance to remove all outbound rules, then add back only the rules required for the application's legitimate external communications
explain: The fastest containment action for an EC2 instance communicating with a known C2 server (when the instance cannot be taken offline) is a NACL deny rule for the specific C2 IP, which operates at the subnet level and blocks the outbound connection immediately. NACLs are stateless and evaluated before security groups, making them effective for immediate traffic blocking. Removing all security group outbound rules would break the application's legitimate external communications. Inspector performs vulnerability scanning and cannot block active network connections.

q: An AWS account shows GuardDuty findings of type 'Recon:IAMUser/UserPermissions' indicating that an IAM entity is calling GetPolicy, GetPolicyVersion, and ListAttachedRolePolicies APIs in rapid succession. No authorized automation is known to perform these calls. What does this finding MOST likely indicate?
options: An Amazon EC2 instance metadata service is refreshing IAM role credentials and checking permissions during instance startup
AWS IAM Access Analyzer is performing its normal automated policy analysis and the calls should be suppressed
An attacker is performing privilege escalation reconnaissance to identify what permissions can be exploited to gain broader access to the AWS account
AWS Config is recording IAM resource configurations as part of its normal configuration history collection
explain: Rapid sequential calls to GetPolicy, GetPolicyVersion, and ListAttachedRolePolicies — IAM read-only APIs — are the signature pattern of privilege escalation reconnaissance, where an attacker who has gained initial access is enumerating available permissions to find a path to higher privileges. GuardDuty's Recon:IAMUser/UserPermissions finding type specifically detects this reconnaissance pattern. AWS Config records resource state changes, not API call patterns, and would not generate this finding.

q: A company has enabled Amazon GuardDuty across all AWS accounts in their organization using delegated administrator. The security team wants to automatically archive GuardDuty findings that are confirmed false positives based on specific resource tags. Which approach achieves this with LEAST operational overhead?
options: Use AWS Security Hub automation rules to suppress findings forwarded from GuardDuty based on resource tag conditions
In each member account, create individual GuardDuty filters for the false positive criteria and set the action to archive
Create an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function to call GuardDuty UpdateFindings API to archive findings matching the tag criteria across all accounts
Create GuardDuty suppression rules in the delegated administrator account that filter findings based on resource tags, which automatically archives matching findings across all member accounts
explain: GuardDuty suppression rules created in the delegated administrator account automatically apply to all member accounts in the organization, archiving matching findings before they are surfaced to security teams. Creating a rule that filters by both finding type and resource tag (containing the specific resource attributes for confirmed false positives) ensures only verified benign resources are suppressed while new resources exhibiting the same behavior still generate findings. Per-member-account rules require N-times the maintenance effort.

q: A security analyst notices GuardDuty findings of type 'CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B!DNS' on multiple EC2 instances in a development environment. Investigation reveals that developers are running cryptocurrency mining software for personal use. The security team needs to prevent this behavior going forward. Which combination of actions should be taken?
options: Deploy AWS WAF rules to block outbound DNS queries to known cryptocurrency mining pool domains on all development instances

Terminate the affected instances, revoke developers' ability to launch instance types with high GPU/CPU counts using IAM policies or SCPs, and implement a GuardDuty suppression rule only for the development account while investigating
Create a GuardDuty suppression rule to archive all CryptoCurrency findings in the development account to reduce alert noise
Enable Amazon Macie on the development account to detect cryptocurrency mining activity and automatically terminate non-compliant instances
explain: When cryptocurrency mining is confirmed as unauthorized developer activity in the development account, the response requires: terminating affected instances (stop the unauthorized mining), implementing preventive controls via IAM/SCP to restrict high-compute instance types (prevent recurrence), and creating a scoped GuardDuty suppression rule only for the specific instances during the remediation period. Suppressing all CryptoCurrency findings account-wide blinds the security team to future mining attempts by other means. Macie and WAF are irrelevant to this threat type.

q: A compliance team requires that all AWS API activity across 50 accounts in an AWS Organization is logged, tamper-proof, and centralized in a dedicated security account for at least 7 years. Which architecture meets these requirements with LEAST operational overhead?
options: Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs with organization-level log data protection policies to centralize logs and configure a 7-year retention period on the log group
Enable AWS Config in all accounts with organization aggregation to the security account, configure S3 lifecycle rules to retain configuration history for 7 years
Create individual CloudTrail trails in each of the 50 accounts and configure them to deliver logs to a centralized S3 bucket in the security account with versioning enabled

Create an organization-level CloudTrail trail in the management account with an S3 bucket in the security account, enable log file integrity validation, and configure S3 Object Lock with a compliance mode retention policy of 7 years
explain: An organization-level CloudTrail trail automatically covers all accounts and regions in a single configuration, delivering logs to a central S3 bucket with log file integrity validation (SHA-256 hashing) ensuring tamper detection. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode prevents any principal — including root — from deleting or modifying logs during the 7-year retention period. Creating individual trails per account requires 50x management effort and risks inconsistent coverage.

q: A security engineer needs to investigate whether any IAM roles were created or modified in the past 90 days across all AWS regions. CloudTrail logging was enabled, but the logs are stored in S3 with a complex prefix structure across multiple folders. Which is the FASTEST way to find this information?
options: Enable AWS Config and run a Config advanced query to find all IAM roles and their creation dates within the 90-day window
Use Amazon Athena to query the CloudTrail S3 bucket, create a table with the CloudTrail log partitions, and run a SQL query filtering for IAM role events
Use AWS CloudTrail Lake to query CreateRole and UpdateAssumeRolePolicy events across all regions using SQL queries within the 90-day event data store window
Write an AWS CLI command using 's3 sync' to download all CloudTrail logs to a local machine, then use 'grep' to search for CreateRole and UpdateAssumeRolePolicy events
explain: CloudTrail Lake provides a fully managed event data store that can be queried directly with SQL without managing S3 partitions, Athena table schemas, or downloading log files. For a time-bound investigation (90 days of IAM role events), CloudTrail Lake's SQL query returns results in seconds. Athena requires creating and maintaining table partitions over the CloudTrail S3 bucket structure. Local grep over downloaded logs is operationally impractical for 90 days of multi-region data.

q: A company must log all Amazon S3 object-level API activity (GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject) for a compliance requirement. The bucket receives 10 million requests per day. The logging solution must have minimal impact on application latency and must not significantly increase S3 costs. Which approach should be used?
options: Enable CloudTrail S3 data event logging for the specific bucket, storing logs in a separate S3 bucket with a lifecycle policy to transition to S3 Glacier after 30 days
Enable S3 server access logging for the bucket and deliver logs to a logging bucket in the same region for analysis
Enable Amazon Macie on the bucket to automatically log all object access events to CloudWatch Logs
Create an S3 Event Notification for all object events (s3:ObjectCreated:*, s3:ObjectRemoved:*) and deliver to an SQS queue for processing
explain: CloudTrail data events for S3 provide an accurate, structured audit trail of every GetObject, PutObject, and DeleteObject call with the IAM identity, timestamp, and object key. An S3 lifecycle policy transitioning logs to Glacier after 30 days reduces long-term storage costs by approximately 75% while maintaining compliance-grade audit records. S3 server access logging is best-effort and may miss some requests; Macie provides data classification, not a complete API access log; S3 event notifications do not capture GetObject reads by default.

q: A security operations center (SOC) needs to correlate VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail logs, and GuardDuty findings in a single place to investigate security incidents. The team wants to run SQL queries across all three data sources. Which AWS service enables this with the LEAST amount of custom development?
options: Enable AWS Security Hub to aggregate GuardDuty findings and CloudTrail Insights, then use Security Hub's custom insights feature to correlate events
Forward GuardDuty findings and CloudTrail events to Amazon OpenSearch Service using EventBridge, configure VPC Flow Log delivery to OpenSearch, and use Kibana for correlation
Configure all three data sources to stream to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, use a Lambda function to normalize the data, write to an Amazon S3 data lake, and query with Athena
Send all three data sources to Amazon Security Lake, which normalizes them to OCSF format and stores them in S3, then use Amazon Athena to run SQL queries across all sources
explain: Amazon Security Lake is purpose-built for security data aggregation and normalization. It automatically ingests VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings, and other sources, normalizes all data to the Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework (OCSF), and stores it in S3 partitioned for Athena queries. This native integration requires minimal custom development compared to building a custom Kinesis + Lambda + S3 pipeline, which requires schema mapping, error handling, and ongoing maintenance.

q: A company's security policy requires that all Amazon EC2 instances in production can only communicate with pre-approved IP ranges and must not be accessible directly from the internet. A SysOps administrator must enforce this and detect any violations automatically. Which solution provides both enforcement and detection?
options: Place all production instances behind a Network Load Balancer with internet-facing listener, restrict security groups to NLB source IPs only, and enable VPC Flow Logs for detection
Use AWS Firewall Manager with a VPC security group policy to enforce a baseline security group and deploy an AWS WAF web ACL on all instances

Deploy all production instances in private subnets with no internet gateway route, use Security Groups with explicit approved CIDR ranges, and create an AWS Config rule 'restricted-ssh' and 'vpc-sg-open-only-to-authorized-ports' with SNS alerting for violations
Enable Amazon Inspector on all production instances to scan for open ports and automatically close unauthorized ports when found
explain: Private subnets without a route to an internet gateway physically prevent inbound internet traffic from reaching EC2 instances. Security groups with only the approved CIDR ranges control authorized communications. AWS Config managed rules (restricted-ssh, vpc-sg-open-only-to-authorized-ports) continuously evaluate security groups and trigger SNS alerts on violations, providing continuous detection. An internet-facing NLB still exposes instances to internet traffic through the load balancer, defeating the isolation purpose.

q: A security team must ensure that all data transmitted between AWS services within their VPC never traverses the public internet. The application uses Amazon S3, Amazon DynamoDB, and AWS Systems Manager. Which solution ensures all service communication stays within the AWS network?
options: Create VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB, and VPC Interface Endpoints for Systems Manager (ssm, ssmmessages, ec2messages), then update route tables and security groups to route all service traffic through the endpoints
Enable VPC Flow Logs and create a CloudWatch Alarm to detect any traffic destined for public IP ranges from within the VPC, then block those routes
Enable AWS PrivateLink for all services by contacting AWS Support to configure private routing at the network layer between the VPC and AWS service endpoints
Place all EC2 instances in private subnets and disable internet gateway access — traffic to S3 and DynamoDB will automatically route through the AWS internal network
explain: VPC Endpoints are the mechanism for private AWS service communication. Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB modify route tables to route traffic through AWS's internal network. Interface Endpoints (powered by PrivateLink) for Systems Manager create private ENIs in the subnet, providing private DNS resolution. Without these endpoints, EC2 instances in private subnets would need a NAT Gateway and internet routing to reach S3, DynamoDB, and Systems Manager. Three Interface Endpoints are required for Systems Manager: ssm, ssmmessages, and ec2messages.

q: A company runs a multi-tier web application in a VPC. The security team discovers that a vulnerability scan found open port 22 (SSH) on all EC2 instances accessible from 0.0.0.0/0 in the security groups. The team has 200 instances across 15 security groups. What is the MOST efficient way to remediate this across all security groups at scale?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Automation with the document AWS-DisablePublicAccessForSecurityGroup to remove the open rules from each security group one at a time
Use AWS Firewall Manager with a Security Group Content Audit policy to identify and automatically remediate security groups that allow unrestricted SSH access across the VPC
Create an AWS Config rule 'restricted-ssh', enable auto-remediation using a Lambda function that revokes the open SSH rule from all non-compliant security groups
Write an AWS CLI script using 'aws ec2 describe-security-groups' to find offending rules and 'aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress' to remove the open SSH rules, then run it manually
explain: AWS Config's 'restricted-ssh' managed rule continuously evaluates all security groups and identifies those with port 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0. With auto-remediation configured via an SSM Automation document (AWS-RevokeSecurityGroupRules), each non-compliant security group is automatically remediated immediately upon detection. This approach continuously monitors and remediates violations, preventing recurrence. A CLI script is one-time and manual; Firewall Manager is better suited for multi-account enforcement.

q: A company uses AWS WAF to protect their Application Load Balancer. The security team observes a SQL injection attack from a rotating set of IP addresses that changes every 15 minutes, making IP-based blocking ineffective. Which WAF feature should be configured to block this attack pattern regardless of source IP?
options: Create a WAF rate-based rule limiting requests to 100 per 5-minute window per IP, which will throttle the attacker's requests
Enable AWS Shield Advanced and configure a custom DDoS response to block traffic patterns matching SQL injection signatures
Create a WAF rule with a SQL injection match condition on the query string, body, and URI components using the AWS Managed Rules SQL Database rule group
Configure WAF with a geo-match rule to block traffic from countries where the attack IPs have been observed
explain: SQL injection match conditions in AWS WAF inspect the content of HTTP requests (query strings, body, URI, and headers) for SQL injection patterns, blocking them regardless of the source IP address. The AWS Managed Rules SQL Database rule group contains regularly updated signatures for known SQL injection techniques. This content-aware detection defeats IP rotation attacks because the attack pattern in the request body is blocked regardless of where the request originates.

q: An organization uses AWS Organizations and wants to prevent any member account from disabling AWS CloudTrail logging or deleting CloudTrail trails. The restriction must apply to all accounts including those with administrator privileges. Which solution enforces this?
options: Create a Service Control Policy (SCP) that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, and cloudtrail:UpdateTrail actions and attach it to the organization root
Enable AWS Config with the 'cloud-trail-enabled' rule in each account and configure auto-remediation to re-enable CloudTrail if it is disabled
Create an IAM permissions boundary that denies CloudTrail modification actions and attach it to all IAM roles in every member account
Enable AWS Security Hub with the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard and configure the CloudTrail controls to auto-remediate violations
explain: SCPs are the only control mechanism that applies to all IAM principals in member accounts including account administrators, and attaching a deny SCP for cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail to the organization root ensures no account can disable or delete CloudTrail. AWS Config auto-remediation is reactive — it detects disabling after it happens and re-enables CloudTrail, creating a brief gap. IAM permission boundaries apply to IAM entities only, not to the root user.

q: A company has a Lambda function that needs to read from an Amazon S3 bucket in Account A and write results to a DynamoDB table in Account B. The Lambda function runs in Account B. How should the security engineer configure IAM to grant the Lambda function access to the S3 bucket in Account A with LEAST privilege?
options: Create an IAM user in Account A with S3 read permissions, generate access keys, and store them as Lambda environment variables in Account B
Create a VPC peering connection between Account A and Account B VPCs and access S3 via a VPC endpoint in Account A's VPC from Account B's Lambda
Attach an S3 bucket policy in Account A granting the Lambda execution role ARN from Account B direct s3:GetObject permission on the bucket

Create an IAM role in Account A with S3 read permissions and a trust policy allowing Account B's Lambda execution role to assume it; configure the Lambda function to assume this role using STS before accessing S3
explain: For cross-account S3 access where a Lambda function in Account B needs to read from Account A, the simplest approach is an S3 bucket policy in Account A granting s3:GetObject to the Lambda's execution role ARN from Account B. The Lambda's existing execution role (Account B) authenticates to S3 via AWS SigV4, and the bucket policy in Account A authorizes the cross-account read without requiring any additional STS AssumeRole calls. Creating IAM users with access keys is a security anti-pattern for service-to-service access.

q: A developer accidentally committed AWS access keys to a public GitHub repository. The security team was notified by AWS. What is the correct order of remediation steps?
options: Create an explicit IAM deny policy for the compromised access key ID to block further usage while investigating, then rotate the keys after the investigation completes
Immediately delete the IAM user associated with the keys to revoke all access, then recreate the user with new keys and notify the developer

Immediately deactivate the compromised access keys in IAM, review CloudTrail for any unauthorized API calls made using those keys, rotate other potentially compromised credentials, then delete the keys after investigation
Delete the GitHub commit history to remove the exposed keys, then rotate the access keys in IAM, and notify the security team to close the alert
explain: The correct credential compromise response sequence is: (1) Deactivate (not delete) the keys immediately to stop ongoing unauthorized API calls while preserving forensic traceability. (2) Review CloudTrail for all API calls made with the compromised keys to determine the breach scope. (3) Rotate other potentially exposed credentials. (4) Delete the keys after investigation is complete. Deleting the GitHub commit history does not remove keys from cached views, forks, or external scanners that may have already captured the credentials.

q: A security team wants to ensure that no IAM role in any account within their AWS Organization can be used to create new IAM users. However, specific accounts in an 'ExemptOU' require this capability. Which solution achieves this with LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create an SCP denying iam:CreateUser and attach it to all OUs except ExemptOU individually; leave ExemptOU without the restrictive SCP
Create IAM permission boundaries on all roles in non-exempt accounts that deny iam:CreateUser, and exclude accounts in ExemptOU from the boundary requirement
Use AWS Config with a custom rule that detects iam:CreateUser API calls and triggers an automated response to revert user creation in non-exempt accounts

Create an SCP denying iam:CreateUser and attach it to the organization root; create a separate SCP allowing iam:CreateUser and attach it to the ExemptOU — the allow SCP at the OU level overrides the root deny for that OU
explain: SCPs use a deny-wins model where an explicit deny at a higher level cannot be overridden by an allow at a lower level. Attaching a deny SCP to the organization root would prevent ExemptOU accounts from creating IAM users even with an allow SCP at the OU level, because the root deny takes precedence. The correct approach is to attach the deny SCP only to the non-exempt OUs, leaving ExemptOU without the restriction. This requires targeted SCP attachment but correctly implements the requirement.

q: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) to provide access to 30 AWS accounts. A security audit finds that several permission sets grant 'AdministratorAccess' to developers. The security team wants to move to least-privilege but needs to understand what permissions developers actually use before restricting them. Which AWS service provides this information?
options: Use AWS Trusted Advisor to analyze IAM permissions across all 30 accounts and identify unused permissions for removal
Use AWS Cost Explorer to identify which services developers have used based on billing data and restrict permissions to only those services
Use IAM Access Analyzer to generate IAM policies based on CloudTrail activity for each account, reviewing the last 90 days of access to identify what actions developers actually performed
Enable Amazon Macie on all accounts to identify what data services developers have accessed and generate least-privilege recommendations
explain: IAM Access Analyzer's policy generation feature analyzes up to 90 days of CloudTrail activity for a specific IAM entity and generates a least-privilege policy based on the actual API calls made. This data-driven approach produces more accurate least-privilege policies than manual code review (which may miss edge cases or conditional code paths) and more specific policies than AWS managed read-only policies (which are still broader than needed for most Lambda functions).

q: An EC2 instance running a web application needs to access AWS Secrets Manager to retrieve database credentials at startup. The application is deployed in an Auto Scaling group and new instances must automatically have access without any manual configuration. What is the MOST secure way to grant this access?
options: Create an IAM user with secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission, store the access keys in the secret itself, and have instances retrieve the secret using a bootstrap access key
Configure the Secrets Manager secret with a resource policy allowing all EC2 instances in the VPC to access it based on the VPC ID condition
Store the Secrets Manager ARN and IAM access keys in the EC2 user data script so each new instance can authenticate to retrieve the secret
