q: A company wants to detect when any new IAM role is created with a trust policy that allows an external AWS account to assume it. This could indicate an unauthorized backdoor being created. The detection must occur within 1 minute. Which solution meets this requirement?
options: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule matching CloudTrail 'CreateRole' events, trigger a Lambda function that parses the trust policy in the event for external account principals, and sends an SNS alert if an external account is found in the trust policy
Use Amazon GuardDuty's IAMUser/AnomalousBehavior finding to detect unusual CreateRole API calls and alert the security team
Enable AWS Config with a custom rule that evaluates every new IAM role's trust policy and triggers SNS if an external account is in the trust relationship
Schedule a Lambda function every minute to call iam:ListRoles and iam:GetRole for new roles and check trust policies for external accounts
explain: EventBridge captures the CreateRole CloudTrail event within seconds and delivers the full event payload (including the AssumeRolePolicyDocument) to a Lambda function. The Lambda can parse the trust policy JSON to check if any Principal contains an account ID other than the current account's ID, and send an SNS notification if an external account is found. AWS Config evaluations have a typical delay of 2-10 minutes after configuration change detection, insufficient for the 1-minute requirement. GuardDuty detects access pattern anomalies, not specific configuration structure.

q: A company's Amazon ECS tasks running on AWS Fargate need to securely retrieve database credentials from AWS Secrets Manager at startup. The tasks run in a private subnet with no internet gateway. What configuration is required to allow the ECS tasks to access Secrets Manager?
options: Enable VPC peering between the private subnet VPC and the Secrets Manager VPC to allow direct private access to the Secrets Manager service
Store the database credentials as ECS task environment variables encrypted with KMS, eliminating the need for Secrets Manager access from the private subnet
Configure a NAT Gateway in a public subnet and route private subnet traffic through it so ECS tasks can reach the public Secrets Manager endpoint

Create a VPC Interface Endpoint for Secrets Manager in the private subnet, ensure the ECS task execution role has secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission for the specific secret ARN, and allow HTTPS (port 443) in the endpoint's security group
explain: A VPC Interface Endpoint for Secrets Manager creates a private DNS entry and ENI in the private subnet, enabling ECS Fargate tasks to call the Secrets Manager API over the private AWS network without any internet routing. The ECS task execution role must have secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission for the specific secret ARN, and the endpoint's security group must allow HTTPS (443) from the ECS task's security group. Secrets Manager is not a separate VPC — it's a regional API endpoint that requires either internet access or an Interface Endpoint for private subnet access.

q: A company runs an application that receives and processes PAN data (payment card numbers). The application must transmit the data over HTTPS and store only the last 4 digits for display purposes. The application is deployed on Amazon ECS and stores data in Amazon RDS. Which implementation is PCI DSS compliant?
options: Hash the full PAN using SHA-256 and store the hash in RDS — users can verify their card by hashing their input and comparing to the stored hash
Store the full PAN in RDS encrypted with SSE-KMS and display only the last 4 digits in the UI by masking in the application presentation layer

Use ACM to provision a TLS certificate for the application endpoint, truncate the PAN to the last 4 digits in application code before storing in RDS, enable RDS encryption with a KMS CMK, and never log the full PAN in application logs
Use Amazon Macie to detect and automatically redact PAN data before it is stored in RDS and log all redaction events to CloudTrail
explain: PCI DSS-compliant PAN handling requires: HTTPS (ACM certificate) for transit encryption, truncation (not hashing) of the PAN to the last 4 digits before storage (PCI DSS explicitly allows storing the last 4 digits), RDS encryption with KMS CMK for at-rest protection, and strict prohibition on logging full PANs. Storing the full PAN even encrypted requires significant additional PCI DSS controls beyond just encryption. SHA-256 hashing is not PCI DSS-accepted for PAN storage since hash collisions and rainbow tables are theoretical risks.

q: A company wants to allow a third-party vendor to audit their AWS resources by reading configuration data. The vendor has their own AWS account. The company wants to grant the minimum permissions required for auditing and ensure the vendor cannot perform any write operations. The access should automatically expire after 30 days. Which approach is correct?
options: Create a temporary IAM user for the vendor, generate access keys, set an account password expiry of 30 days, and provide the keys to the vendor

Create an IAM role in the company's account with a trust policy allowing the vendor's AWS account ID to assume it, attach read-only managed policies (SecurityAudit, ReadOnlyAccess), and set an STS session duration maximum; notify the vendor they can assume the role and plan a manual review at 30 days
Enable AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share all auditable resources with the vendor's account for 30 days and revoke the share afterward
Share the company's AWS account root credentials with the vendor for 30 days, then rotate the root password after the audit is complete
explain: Cross-account IAM role assumption is the standard pattern for third-party auditor access: the company creates a role with read-only managed policies (SecurityAudit, ReadOnlyAccess) and a trust policy allowing only the vendor's specific account ID. The vendor uses STS AssumeRole to get temporary credentials (1-12 hour sessions) that automatically expire. The role can be deleted after 30 days to revoke access. Creating IAM users creates long-term credentials; root credential sharing is a severe security violation.

q: A company uses AWS Organizations and wants to ensure that all CloudTrail trails created in member accounts are not deleted or modified by account administrators. They also want to create a new organization-level trail that captures all accounts. Which two actions should they take?
options: Create individual CloudTrail trails in each of the 50 accounts and use AWS Config conformance packs to enforce CloudTrail is always enabled

Create an organization CloudTrail trail in the management account with a dedicated S3 bucket; attach an SCP to the organization root denying cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, cloudtrail:StopLogging, and cloudtrail:UpdateTrail for all member accounts
Enable AWS Security Hub CloudTrail controls across all accounts and configure Security Hub auto-remediation to re-enable CloudTrail if disabled
Create an organization CloudTrail trail and configure AWS Config in each member account to detect CloudTrail changes and auto-remediate by re-enabling the trail
explain: An organization CloudTrail trail automatically captures all API activity from all member accounts without per-account trail configuration. An SCP denying cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, cloudtrail:StopLogging, and cloudtrail:UpdateTrail at the org root prevents account administrators (who are subject to SCPs) from modifying any trail. This two-action combination provides both comprehensive coverage and tamper protection. AWS Config auto-remediation is reactive — it allows a brief logging gap before re-enabling CloudTrail.

q: A security team notices GuardDuty findings of type 'Discovery:S3/TorIPCaller' showing that a Tor exit node is accessing S3 objects in a specific bucket. The bucket contains public documentation intended for general access. The security team needs to block Tor access while maintaining public access for legitimate users. What should they do?
options: Enable S3 Block Public Access on the bucket to prevent all anonymous access, including from Tor exit nodes

Create an AWS WAF IP set rule using the Tor exit node list (managed by AWS or a custom threat intelligence list) and attach it to the CloudFront distribution serving the S3 content, blocking requests from Tor IPs while allowing all other public access
Create an S3 bucket policy with an explicit Deny for all Tor exit node IP ranges using aws:SourceIp conditions
Create a GuardDuty suppression rule for the 'Discovery:S3/TorIPCaller' finding type since the bucket is intentionally public
explain: AWS WAF with a managed Tor exit node IP reputation list (or a custom IP set containing Tor exit node IPs) blocks requests from Tor at the CloudFront edge before they reach the S3 origin, preserving public access for all non-Tor users. This is the precise, selective blocking mechanism needed. S3 Block Public Access removes all public access. Suppressing the GuardDuty finding without blocking Tor access hides the activity without mitigating it. A bucket policy with all Tor IPs requires maintaining a constantly changing list of thousands of IPs.

q: A company must ensure that encryption keys used for Amazon EBS volumes are automatically rotated every 90 days (not the standard 365-day annual rotation). AWS KMS automatic rotation only supports annual rotation. How should the company implement 90-day key rotation?
options: Use SSE-S3 encryption for EBS volumes instead of KMS — SSE-S3 automatically rotates key material at any interval configured by the customer

Implement a custom key rotation solution: create a Lambda function triggered by an EventBridge rule every 90 days that creates a new KMS CMK, re-encrypts the EBS snapshot using aws:kms:ReEncrypt, creates a new volume from the re-encrypted snapshot, and updates the EC2 instance to use the new volume
Enable KMS automatic key rotation on all CMKs — although it defaults to 365 days, the rotation period can be adjusted to 90 days in the KMS console
Use AWS CloudHSM with a custom application that generates new keys every 90 days and re-encrypts EBS volume data using the new key material
explain: AWS KMS automatic key rotation only supports annual (365-day) rotation intervals and cannot be configured for shorter periods. To achieve 90-day rotation, a custom process is required: an EventBridge scheduled rule triggers a Lambda function every 90 days that creates a new CMK, re-encrypts EBS snapshots using kms:ReEncrypt, creates new volumes from the re-encrypted snapshots, and updates EC2 instances to use the new volumes. EBS volumes do not use SSE-S3 encryption; they exclusively use KMS.

q: A financial company runs a highly sensitive workload on Amazon EC2. The security team wants to ensure that no one, including AWS employees, can access the memory or storage of these instances during operation or after termination. Which EC2 capability provides this level of protection?
options: Use EC2 instances with Nitro Enclaves and AWS Nitro System — Nitro-based instances provide cryptographic attestation and hardware isolation where even AWS operators cannot access instance memory, storage, or CPU state
Enable EBS encryption with a KMS CMK that has a key policy denying AWS employees, and use VPC security groups to prevent any AWS service from accessing the instance
Enable EC2 Hibernation and store the instance memory state in an encrypted EBS volume with a KMS CMK that AWS employees cannot access
Use Dedicated Hosts to ensure the physical server is exclusively for the company's use, preventing AWS from placing other customers' workloads on the same hardware
explain: The AWS Nitro System is specifically designed with a no-operator-access architectural guarantee: the Nitro hypervisor and supporting hardware are built so that AWS employees cannot access customer instance memory, storage, or CPU state — even under court order. Nitro Enclaves extend this with cryptographic attestation for isolated compute environments. KMS key policies can restrict IAM access to encryption keys but do not address physical infrastructure access. Dedicated Hosts prevent hardware sharing with other customers but do not restrict AWS operator access.

q: A company's security policy requires that all API calls to AWS services that modify IAM resources must generate a separate immutable audit log that is stored separately from the main CloudTrail log and cannot be deleted by account administrators. How should this be implemented?
options: Create a secondary CloudTrail trail that filters for IAM events only, deliver logs to an S3 bucket in a separate logging account, enable S3 Object Lock with compliance mode on the logging bucket, and restrict the account administrator from accessing the logging account
Enable AWS Config recording for IAM resources and export Config configuration history to an S3 bucket with MFA delete enabled
Create an EventBridge rule matching all IAM API events and archive them in EventBridge's event archive feature with indefinite retention
Create a CloudWatch Metric Filter on CloudTrail logs matching IAM events and export matching events to a CloudWatch Logs Insights query saved to S3
explain: A secondary CloudTrail trail with a filter for IAM management events delivers logs to an S3 bucket in a separate logging account. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode in the logging account makes the IAM audit logs immutable — neither the main account administrator nor the logging account root can delete or modify logs during the retention period. EventBridge archives are not designed for compliance-grade immutable log storage; CloudWatch Logs insights exports are not immutable.

q: A company discovers that several Amazon S3 buckets have object-level logging disabled. AWS Config's 's3-bucket-logging-enabled' rule marks these as non-compliant. The security team wants automatic remediation that enables S3 server access logging on all non-compliant buckets immediately. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Use Amazon Macie to detect buckets without logging and configure Macie to automatically enable logging on non-compliant buckets
Create a Lambda function that calls s3:PutBucketLogging on all buckets and schedule it via EventBridge to run every 15 minutes
Use CloudFormation Stack Drift Detection to identify buckets without logging enabled and trigger a stack update to add logging configuration

In AWS Config, configure the 's3-bucket-logging-enabled' rule with an SSM Automation remediation action using the 'AWS-EnableS3BucketLogging' document, specifying a target logging bucket and prefix, and enable automatic remediation
explain: AWS Config's 's3-bucket-logging-enabled' rule with auto-remediation via the 'AWS-EnableS3BucketLogging' SSM Automation document triggers immediately when a bucket is evaluated as non-compliant, calling the S3 API to enable server access logging on the specific bucket. This is the native, no-custom-code solution for continuous S3 logging compliance enforcement. A scheduled Lambda is less efficient (not event-driven) and requires custom development. Macie cannot enable S3 logging.

q: A company's GuardDuty generates a finding 'Impact:EC2/AbusedDomainRequest.Reputation'. The finding shows that an EC2 instance in a production VPC is making DNS queries to domains with low reputation scores. The security team wants to validate whether this is legitimate business traffic or a compromise. What is the MOST efficient first step?
options: Review the specific domain names in the GuardDuty finding details, query the domains against threat intelligence databases (e.g., VirusTotal, Shodan) to determine if they are known malicious, and cross-reference with the application's known external dependencies
Immediately isolate the EC2 instance by revoking all security group rules and creating a NACL blocking all traffic to and from the instance
Enable Amazon Macie on the instance to determine if any sensitive data was transmitted to the low-reputation domains
Run an Amazon Inspector vulnerability scan on the instance to determine if any exploited vulnerability is causing the malicious domain queries
explain: Amazon GuardDuty's Impact:EC2/AbusedDomainRequest.Reputation finding triggers on DNS queries to domains with low reputation scores, which could be false positives (new or obscure but legitimate vendors). The first step is always triage: review the specific domain names, cross-reference with threat intelligence databases (VirusTotal, URLhaus), and verify against the application's known external dependencies list. Immediate isolation without triage causes unnecessary service disruption for a potentially legitimate finding.

q: A company uses AWS Single Sign-On (IAM Identity Center) and their corporate Active Directory as the identity source. A developer group needs read-only access to EC2 and S3 in development accounts but full access in their personal sandbox accounts. The access must be managed centrally. How should permission sets be configured?
options: Create two permission sets — 'Dev-ReadOnly' (EC2ReadOnly + S3ReadOnly) and 'Dev-FullAccess' (AmazonEC2FullAccess + AmazonS3FullAccess) — assign 'Dev-ReadOnly' to the developer group for development accounts and 'Dev-FullAccess' for sandbox accounts in IAM Identity Center
Use IAM Identity Center attribute-based access control (ABAC) with user attributes from Active Directory to control permission levels per account
Create a single permission set with an IAM condition that grants full access when the account ID matches sandbox accounts and read-only access otherwise
Create separate Active Directory groups for sandbox and development access, mapping each to a different IAM Identity Center permission set via group assignments
explain: IAM Identity Center allows different permission sets to be assigned to the same group for different accounts. Creating 'Dev-ReadOnly' and 'Dev-FullAccess' permission sets and assigning them to the developer group for the appropriate account types is centrally managed in IAM Identity Center without per-account IAM group creation. A single permission set with account-based conditions is not a supported IAM Identity Center feature. Separate AD groups for each access level adds AD management overhead.

q: A company must implement field-level encryption for credit card numbers stored in Amazon DynamoDB. Only a specific Lambda function responsible for payment processing should be able to decrypt the card numbers. All other services that read DynamoDB should see the encrypted ciphertext. Which implementation achieves this?
options: Use AWS KMS to encrypt the credit card field in the payment Lambda function before writing to DynamoDB, with a KMS key policy that grants kms:Decrypt only to the payment Lambda's execution role; all other services reading DynamoDB will see ciphertext and cannot decrypt without the key permission
Use Amazon Macie to detect credit card numbers in DynamoDB and automatically encrypt them using SSE-DynamoDB encryption
Enable DynamoDB field-level encryption in the table settings to encrypt specific attributes, configuring the payment Lambda as the only authorized decryptor
Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with a KMS CMK scoped to only the payment Lambda's execution role, preventing all other services from reading any DynamoDB data
explain: Application-level (field-level) encryption using KMS is the correct approach for DynamoDB attribute-level encryption: the Lambda function encrypts the sensitive field value using KMS GenerateDataKey, stores the ciphertext in the DynamoDB attribute. A KMS key policy granting kms:Decrypt only to the payment Lambda's execution role ensures only that function can decrypt the field. DynamoDB does not natively support field-level encryption — table-level KMS encryption protects all attributes equally, not individual sensitive fields.

q: A company wants to implement a Zero Trust network architecture for their AWS workloads. Services should authenticate each other using identity rather than network location, and each service should only be able to communicate with explicitly authorized services. Which AWS capabilities should be used to implement this?
options: Implement strict security group rules between all services, allowing only the specific ports and IP ranges needed for communication
Use VPC peering between all service VPCs with NACLs restricting traffic to only authorized subnet CIDR ranges

Use AWS App Mesh with mutual TLS (mTLS) service authentication using certificates from AWS Certificate Manager Private CA, combined with service-specific IAM roles and resource policies to authorize inter-service API calls based on identity
Enable VPC Flow Logs and use GuardDuty to detect unauthorized service-to-service communication, automatically blocking it via NACL updates
explain: AWS App Mesh with mutual TLS (mTLS) using certificates from ACM Private CA authenticates service identities cryptographically rather than relying on network location (IP address or VPC). Each service has an identity certificate; mTLS handshakes verify both parties before establishing connections. IAM resource policies enforce authorization at the API level. Security groups based on IP/port are network-perimeter controls — in Zero Trust architecture, network location should not imply trust.

q: A multinational company has AWS accounts in multiple regions. Compliance requirements mandate that no data leaves specific geographic regions (EU data must stay in EU regions). The company wants an automated guardrail that prevents EC2 instances, S3 buckets, and other resources from being created in non-approved regions. Which control achieves this?
options: Create an AWS Config rule 'approved-amis-by-id' in each EU account to prevent EC2 from launching in non-approved regions
Enable Amazon Macie in all accounts to detect data in non-approved regions and automatically delete the data

Create an SCP with a deny statement for all resource creation actions conditioned on aws:RequestedRegion not being in the approved region list, and attach it to the EU accounts OU in AWS Organizations
Configure IAM permission boundaries on all IAM roles in EU accounts to deny resource creation in non-approved regions
explain: An SCP with aws:RequestedRegion conditions denies all resource-creating API calls when the requested region is not in the approved EU region list (eu-west-1, eu-central-1, etc.). Attaching this to the EU accounts OU prevents any principal — including account administrators — from creating resources outside approved regions. This satisfies GDPR data residency requirements as a preventive, organization-wide control. IAM permission boundaries require per-role application and don't cover the root user.

q: A company enables GuardDuty in all regions. A finding 'UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/SSHBruteForce' appears for an EC2 instance. The instance is an internet-facing bastion host that legitimately receives SSH connections. The security team wants to stop the brute force attack without blocking legitimate SSH access. Which response is MOST appropriate?
options: Enable Amazon Inspector on the bastion host to detect the brute force and automatically block the attacking IP
Immediately remove the 0.0.0.0/0 inbound SSH rule from the bastion host's security group to block all external SSH access

Configure AWS WAF with a rate-based rule for the SSH port (note: WAF is HTTP/S only); instead, use a GuardDuty-triggered Lambda function that updates the security group to block the attacker's IP while preserving access from the corporate CIDR range
Create a GuardDuty suppression rule for 'UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/SSHBruteForce' on the bastion host since SSH brute force is expected for internet-facing hosts
explain: A GuardDuty-triggered Lambda function is the correct approach: EventBridge routes the finding to Lambda, which extracts the attacker's source IP from the finding details, adds it to a security group deny rule or NACL, while preserving the existing allow rules for corporate IP ranges. AWS WAF operates only on HTTP/HTTPS traffic and cannot block raw TCP/SSH connections. Removing all external SSH rules blocks legitimate users. Suppressing the finding without mitigation leaves the attack running.

q: A company must log all AWS Management Console sign-in events, including failed attempts, and alert the security team when more than 5 failed sign-ins occur for a single user within 10 minutes. Which solution implements this with LEAST operational overhead?
options: CloudTrail logs all console sign-in events (ConsoleLogin) to CloudWatch Logs; create a Metric Filter counting Failed ConsoleLogin events per username, create a CloudWatch Alarm with a 10-minute period and threshold of 5, and configure SNS notification
Enable AWS Security Hub and create a custom insight that counts failed ConsoleLogin events from CloudTrail and alerts when the threshold is exceeded
Create a Lambda function triggered by EventBridge every 5 minutes that queries CloudTrail Event History for failed sign-ins and sends alerts via SNS
Enable Amazon Cognito advanced security features with compromise action detection to track failed sign-ins and send SNS notifications
explain: CloudTrail captures ConsoleLogin events with the ResponseElements.ConsoleLogin field indicating 'Failure' for failed attempts. A CloudWatch Metric Filter on the CloudTrail log group counts failures per username (using the userIdentity.userName dimension), and a CloudWatch Alarm with a 10-minute evaluation period and threshold of 5 triggers an SNS notification. This is the native, lowest-overhead solution. Cognito handles application-level authentication, not AWS console sign-ins.

q: A company runs a multi-tier application in a VPC with public subnets (ALB) and private subnets (application servers). A security audit finds that the application servers have security group rules allowing all outbound traffic (0.0.0.0/0). The security team wants to restrict outbound traffic to only what is necessary: HTTPS to specific AWS service endpoints, and MySQL to the RDS instance. What is the recommended approach?
options: Deploy an AWS WAF with outbound inspection rules on the application servers to block non-HTTPS and non-MySQL traffic
Use VPC Flow Logs to monitor outbound traffic and create CloudWatch Alarms to alert when traffic goes to unapproved destinations
Create a Network ACL on the private subnet blocking all outbound traffic except HTTPS and MySQL to specified CIDR ranges

Modify the application server security group to remove the all-outbound rule; add specific outbound rules: HTTPS (443) to the VPC endpoint security group for AWS services, and MySQL (3306) to the RDS security group ID
explain: Security group outbound rules should be scoped to specific destinations: HTTPS (443) to the VPC endpoint security group ID (for AWS service communication through interface endpoints) and MySQL (3306) to the RDS instance's security group ID. Referencing security group IDs in outbound rules ensures traffic is allowed only to instances that are members of those groups, not to arbitrary IP ranges. NACLs are subnet-level and stateless, making them less precise for service-to-service outbound controls.

q: A company uses Amazon S3 to store documents. A new requirement states that once a document is finalized (uploaded to a specific S3 prefix '/finalized/'), it must be immutable — no modifications or deletions allowed for 7 years, even by administrators. Which S3 feature implements this?
options: Enable S3 versioning on the bucket and configure MFA Delete — administrators must use MFA to delete any object version, providing sufficient protection

Enable S3 Object Lock with governance mode on objects in the '/finalized/' prefix with a 7-year retention period, and use an S3 Lifecycle rule to automatically set Object Lock on new uploads to this prefix
Create an S3 bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject and s3:PutObject for all principals on the '/finalized/' prefix

Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode on objects uploaded to '/finalized/' with a 7-year retention — this prevents deletion or modification by ANY principal including root during the retention period
explain: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode is the only mechanism that prevents deletion or overwriting by any AWS principal — including root — during the retention period. Governance mode (Option B) allows users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission to override the lock, which does not satisfy the 'even by administrators' requirement. MFA Delete protects object versions but can be bypassed by root. An S3 bucket policy deny can be modified by an admin with sufficient permissions.

q: A company has a compliance requirement to detect any Amazon EC2 security group that allows unrestricted access (0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0) on any port except HTTPS (443) and SSH from the corporate network only. They need continuous monitoring and automatic remediation across 20 AWS accounts. Which solution is MOST appropriate?
options: Use AWS Firewall Manager with a Security Group Content Audit policy configured to flag and optionally auto-remediate security groups that violate the specified rules across all accounts in the organization
Create a custom AWS Config rule in each account that checks security groups and triggers a Lambda function to revoke non-compliant inbound rules automatically
Run a daily AWS CLI script from a central management server that scans all 20 accounts using assume-role and revokes non-compliant security group rules
Enable AWS Security Hub with the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark which includes security group checks, and configure Security Hub to auto-remediate via Security Hub actions
explain: AWS Firewall Manager Security Group Content Audit policies define the allowed and disallowed security group rule content (e.g., no 0.0.0.0/0 inbound on any port except 443) and enforce these rules across all accounts in the organization through a centralized policy. Firewall Manager can automatically remediate non-compliant security group rules by removing them. A custom Config rule in each of the 20 accounts requires 20x deployment and maintenance; a daily CLI script is not continuous monitoring.

q: A security team receives a GuardDuty finding 'Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity.B' for a production EC2 instance. The finding indicates the instance is communicating with a known botnet command-and-control server. The application on the instance serves 10,000 users and cannot be taken offline. What is the recommended IMMEDIATE containment action that minimizes service disruption?
options: Take an EBS snapshot for forensics, terminate the instance immediately, and redirect users to a maintenance page
Suspend the instance using EC2 Instance Stop to halt the C2 communication without terminating it, preserving memory state for forensics
Add a Network ACL deny rule for the C2 server IP address in the instance's subnet while keeping the instance running

Move the instance to a 'quarantine' security group that allows only inbound traffic from users but blocks all outbound internet traffic, preserving service availability while preventing C2 communication; simultaneously launch a replacement instance to take over the workload
explain: A quarantine security group approach provides the best balance: it blocks all outbound internet traffic (stopping C2 communication) while preserving inbound service availability. Simultaneously launching a clean replacement instance provides a path to fully trusted service. Terminating the instance immediately causes a service outage for 10,000 users. A single NACL deny rule for the specific C2 IP is insufficient — the malware can rotate C2 servers. The quarantine approach blocks all unexpected outbound comprehensively.

q: A company's security team performs a quarterly IAM review and finds several IAM roles with the AdministratorAccess policy that haven't been used in 180 days based on the IAM Last Used date. The team wants to automatically detect and alert on IAM roles unused for 90+ days going forward. Which solution achieves this with minimal custom code?
options: Create a scheduled Lambda function that calls iam:ListRoles, checks LastUsedDate for each role, and sends SNS notifications for roles unused 90+ days

Enable AWS IAM Access Analyzer in all accounts with an unused access analyzer — it automatically identifies IAM roles, users, and access keys that haven't been used for the configured number of days and generates findings in Security Hub
Enable AWS Trusted Advisor IAM checks, which identify unused IAM roles based on last activity dates and generate recommendations
Use AWS Config with the 'iam-role-managed-policy-check' rule to detect roles with AdministratorAccess and flag them as non-compliant after 90 days of non-use
explain: IAM Access Analyzer's unused access analyzer automatically tracks unused IAM roles, users, and access keys based on CloudTrail activity data and generates findings when they've been unused beyond a configurable threshold (90+ days). These findings can flow to Security Hub and EventBridge for alerting — no custom Lambda code or credential report parsing required. A custom Lambda can accomplish this but requires development and maintenance. Trusted Advisor identifies some IAM issues but doesn't provide configurable threshold-based unused access monitoring.

q: A company processes sensitive customer data in AWS and requires that all SSL/TLS certificates used for their public-facing applications are automatically renewed before expiry and that the security team is alerted 30 days before any certificate expires. Which AWS service combination meets these requirements?
options: Use self-signed certificates renewed manually every year, with a calendar reminder set 30 days before expiry for the security team

Use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) for certificates on ALBs and CloudFront — ACM automatically renews certificates before expiry; create an EventBridge rule matching ACM certificate renewal failure events and configure an SNS topic to alert 30 days before expiry using the ACM certificate expiry EventBridge event
Store all certificates in AWS Secrets Manager with a rotation function and configure Secrets Manager to send SNS notifications 30 days before the secret's next rotation date
Use Let's Encrypt certificates with a cron job on EC2 to renew every 90 days and send email alerts 30 days before expiry
explain: AWS Certificate Manager automatically renews certificates for ALBs and CloudFront distributions before expiry. ACM publishes EventBridge events at 45, 30, 15, 7, and 1 day before certificate expiry, enabling an EventBridge rule to trigger SNS notification to the security team at exactly the 30-day mark. This is fully automated with zero manual certificate management. Self-signed certificates require manual renewal. Let's Encrypt requires EC2 infrastructure for automated renewal. Secrets Manager stores secrets, not TLS certificates for AWS-managed services.

q: A company's security team wants to implement a formal 'Security Champion' program where specific developers can approve certain security exceptions in non-production accounts but cannot grant exceptions in production accounts. The security team wants to enforce this centrally. How should this be implemented in AWS?
options: Create separate OUs for production and non-production accounts in AWS Organizations; create an SCP for the production OU that denies the specific exception-granting actions; in IAM Identity Center, create a 'SecurityChampion' permission set with the exception-granting actions and assign it only to non-production accounts
Create IAM groups named 'SecurityChampion-Prod' and 'SecurityChampion-NonProd', assigning different IAM policies to each group across all accounts
Enable AWS Control Tower and create a custom guardrail that prevents Security Champions from approving exceptions in accounts tagged as 'Production'
Use IAM tags on the Security Champion users with 'Account-Type: Non-Production' and create IAM policies with aws:PrincipalTag conditions that restrict exception actions in production
explain: The combination of Organizations OUs and IAM Identity Center is the cleanest centralized implementation: a production OU SCP denies the specific exception-granting actions for all principals in production accounts, while the IAM Identity Center SecurityChampion permission set (assigned only to non-production accounts) provides the capability where it is intended. New accounts automatically inherit the correct policy based on their OU placement. Per-account IAM groups require per-account management and don't scale.

q: A company needs to share sensitive financial reports stored in Amazon S3 with board members who do not have AWS accounts. The reports must only be accessible for 72 hours after being sent, and access must be logged for compliance. Which solution meets these requirements with LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create temporary IAM users for each board member with S3 read access, set account password expiry of 72 hours, enable CloudTrail, and delete the users after 72 hours
Temporarily make the S3 objects public for 72 hours with a public access policy, and enable S3 Access Logs for compliance tracking

Generate S3 pre-signed URLs with 72-hour expiration for each report, send the URLs to board members via email, and ensure S3 server access logging (or CloudTrail data events) is enabled to log all GetObject calls to the reports
Deploy an Amazon CloudFront distribution with signed cookies that expire after 72 hours, requiring board members to authenticate via a custom Lambda@Edge function before accessing reports
explain: S3 pre-signed URLs are the lowest-overhead solution: no AWS accounts required for the board members, automatic expiration at exactly 72 hours, and CloudTrail data events (or S3 server access logging) capture every GetObject for compliance. One URL per report, shared via email with no infrastructure changes. Temporary IAM users add management overhead. CloudFront signed cookies require a distribution, Lambda@Edge authentication, and are significant over-engineering for this simple use case.

q: A company enables Amazon GuardDuty and Amazon Macie in their AWS account. Both services generate findings about the same S3 bucket. How do GuardDuty and Macie findings differ in what they detect?
options: GuardDuty and Macie detect the same types of findings; enabling both is redundant and one should be disabled to reduce costs
GuardDuty monitors S3 bucket policy changes via CloudTrail; Macie monitors S3 object-level API calls via server access logs
GuardDuty detects public access misconfigurations on S3 buckets; Macie detects encryption compliance issues on S3 objects

GuardDuty detects threat activity (anomalous access patterns, malicious IPs, API call anomalies) against S3 buckets; Macie detects sensitive data content (PII, credentials, financial data) within S3 objects — they complement each other
explain: GuardDuty and Macie complement rather than duplicate each other. GuardDuty's S3 Protection detects threat activity (anomalous API call patterns, access from malicious IPs, unusual data volumes) using behavioral baselines. Macie scans S3 object content to classify sensitive data types (PII, credentials, financial data) using ML-based content analysis. A bucket can simultaneously have a GuardDuty finding (suspicious access behavior) and a Macie finding (sensitive PII content) — both are needed for complete S3 security.

q: A company uses an AWS Lambda function that requires access to multiple DynamoDB tables and an S3 bucket. A security engineer notices the Lambda function's execution role has a policy with 'Action: dynamodb:*, s3:*' on 'Resource: *'. What is the MOST secure replacement policy structure?
options: Replace with specific actions: dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:PutItem (only the actions the function calls) on the specific table ARNs; s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject on the specific bucket and prefix ARN (arn:aws:s3:::bucket-name/prefix/*)
Replace with a single policy statement using 'Action: *' on specific resource ARNs, which limits the blast radius to only those resources
Keep the wildcard actions but restrict to specific resources: 'Resource: arn:aws:dynamodb:*:*:table/specific-table' and 'Resource: arn:aws:s3:::specific-bucket/*'
Replace with AWS managed policies AmazonDynamoDBReadOnlyAccess and AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess as a quick least-privilege improvement
explain: True least-privilege IAM policy requires both action-level specificity (only the exact API calls the function makes: dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:PutItem, not dynamodb:*) and resource-level specificity (only the exact table ARNs and S3 bucket/prefix ARNs). Using IAM Access Analyzer to generate this policy from the function's 90-day CloudTrail activity provides evidence-based precision. AWS managed read-only policies are still over-permissive (include Scan, Query, and other unused actions). Resource-only restriction with wildcard actions still allows destructive operations like DeleteTable.

q: A company runs a security incident response program. When a GuardDuty finding of HIGH severity is generated, the security team must be notified within 1 minute, and an automated isolation workflow must start within 2 minutes. Which architecture implements this?
options: Configure GuardDuty to directly trigger AWS Systems Manager Automation documents for high-severity findings without any intermediary services
Enable GuardDuty findings export to S3, create an S3 event notification to a Lambda function that checks severity and triggers notifications and isolation for high-severity findings

Create an EventBridge rule matching GuardDuty findings with severity >= 7.0, trigger two targets simultaneously: an SNS topic for immediate team notification and an AWS Step Functions state machine that orchestrates the isolation workflow (security group update, snapshot, forensic tagging)
Create a GuardDuty CloudWatch Alarm for high-severity findings, configure the alarm to trigger an SNS notification and a Lambda function that isolates the resource
explain: EventBridge is the native integration point for GuardDuty, receiving findings within seconds of generation. Using a single EventBridge rule with two simultaneous targets — SNS for immediate notification and Step Functions for orchestrated isolation workflow — satisfies both the 1-minute notification and 2-minute isolation requirements. Step Functions provides the complex orchestration (security group update, EBS snapshot, forensic tagging, ITSM ticket creation) with built-in error handling and retry logic. GuardDuty cannot directly trigger SSM Automation documents without EventBridge.

q: A company's DevOps team accidentally stores a private SSH key in a public S3 bucket. Amazon Macie generates a finding. What is the correct sequence of remediation actions?
options: Immediately revoke/delete the exposed SSH key from all systems where it was authorized; block public access on the S3 bucket; delete or overwrite the exposed key file from S3; audit server access logs to check if the key was accessed by unauthorized parties
Delete the S3 bucket immediately to remove the exposed key, then regenerate all SSH keys across all systems
Create an S3 bucket policy denying public access to the specific object path and notify the DevOps team to use a different bucket for future uploads
Rotate the S3 bucket's KMS key to prevent further access to the exposed key file
explain: The correct incident response sequence for an exposed SSH private key: (1) Immediately revoke the key from authorized_keys on all servers — this is the critical containment step that stops unauthorized SSH access. (2) Block public S3 access to stop further exposure. (3) Delete the file from S3 to remove the exposure artifact. (4) Review S3 server access logs or CloudTrail data events to determine if the key was downloaded by unauthorized parties. Rotating the S3 bucket KMS key doesn't help — the object was publicly readable without KMS decryption needed.

q: A company uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for all EC2 instance access, eliminating SSH bastion hosts. A compliance audit requires that all Session Manager sessions are logged and that the logs cannot be tampered with by system administrators. Which configuration satisfies the audit requirement?
options: Enable VPC Flow Logs on all instance subnets to capture the network activity during Session Manager sessions
Require IAM users to enable session recording individually before each session to ensure self-compliance
Enable Session Manager session logging to a local file on each EC2 instance and collect the logs via CloudWatch Agent

Configure Session Manager to stream session activity to an S3 bucket with Object Lock (compliance mode) and to CloudWatch Logs, with S3 bucket and CloudWatch log group policies preventing deletion by IAM administrators
explain: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager can be configured to stream session content (all commands executed and their output) to S3 and CloudWatch Logs simultaneously through SSM preferences. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode prevents any IAM administrator from deleting or modifying session logs during the retention period. This provides tamper-proof, administrator-resistant audit logging. Local EC2 file logging can be deleted by anyone with OS access. VPC Flow Logs capture network metadata, not session commands.

q: A company must demonstrate to a PCI DSS auditor that all changes to network security controls (security groups, NACLs, route tables) in production AWS accounts are logged and attributed to a specific authorized person. Which combination of AWS services provides this evidence?
options: VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic changes and identify the source IP of the entity making security changes, attributing them to specific users
AWS Config records all security group, NACL, and route table changes with timestamps; combined with AWS Security Hub, this provides the required audit evidence
Amazon CloudWatch Logs aggregates all API activity and provides user-level attribution for network security control changes

AWS CloudTrail captures all API calls with the userIdentity field showing which IAM user or role made each change; combined with IAM Identity Center providing named user attribution through federated access, CloudTrail provides complete who-what-when audit trails for security control changes
explain: CloudTrail captures every API call with the userIdentity field identifying the exact IAM user, role ARN, or federated identity. When IAM Identity Center (SSO) is used, CloudTrail records the SSO user's identity with the federated user ARN, providing named person attribution through the federation mapping. This is the definitive audit source for 'who changed what and when' for PCI DSS network security control change attribution. VPC Flow Logs capture network traffic, not API-level who/what/when.

q: A company wants to implement a 'break-glass' emergency access process for production AWS accounts. In a genuine emergency, a security engineer should be able to assume a highly privileged role, but all break-glass access must be logged and automatically trigger an alert to the CISO. Which design meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Console login with root credentials for break-glass access, which are stored in a physical safe and automatically generate CloudTrail root activity events

Create a break-glass IAM role with AdministratorAccess, restrict who can assume it via a trust policy, create an EventBridge rule that triggers an SNS alert to the CISO when the AssumeRole event for this specific role is detected in CloudTrail
Create a break-glass IAM user with full access, store the credentials in a physical safe, and enable MFA on the user — access attempts generate CloudTrail events that the CISO reviews weekly
Store break-glass credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, grant the security team access to the secret, and configure Secrets Manager rotation to alert the CISO each time the secret is retrieved
explain: The break-glass role pattern provides both auditability and automated alerting: the IAM role requires MFA in the trust policy (ensuring authentication), CloudTrail captures every AssumeRole event with the caller identity and timestamp, and EventBridge matches AssumeRole events for this specific role ARN to trigger immediate SNS notification to the CISO. Root credentials for break-glass is an anti-pattern — root sessions have different audit properties and cannot be scoped with permission policies.

q: A company's GuardDuty generates a finding 'Stealth:IAMUser/CloudTrailLoggingDisabled'. Investigation confirms that an IAM user disabled a CloudTrail trail. What does this finding indicate about the attacker's intent and what should be done immediately?
options: The finding indicates an accidental configuration change; re-enable CloudTrail and provide the IAM user with training on AWS best practices

GuardDuty continues to monitor even when CloudTrail is disabled because it uses VPC Flow Logs and DNS logs as alternative sources; take no immediate action and investigate the finding at the next business day
The attacker has already exfiltrated all data they needed before disabling CloudTrail; focus investigation on what data was in the account rather than re-enabling logging

Disabling CloudTrail is a common attacker technique to cover tracks and prevent detection of subsequent malicious activity; immediately re-enable CloudTrail, investigate what was done in the window when logging was disabled using any available logs, and disable the compromised IAM user's credentials
explain: Disabling CloudTrail is a documented MITRE ATT&CK cloud defense evasion technique. Attackers disable audit logging before performing more damaging actions (data exfiltration, credential creation, lateral movement) to avoid detection. The immediate response is: (1) Re-enable CloudTrail to restore visibility. (2) Investigate the logging gap using GuardDuty findings (which use VPC Flow Logs and DNS as alternative data sources), application logs, and any pre-disabling CloudTrail events. (3) Disable the compromised IAM credentials to stop further access.

q: A company uses multiple AWS accounts managed through AWS Organizations. A developer in account A needs temporary access to read objects from an S3 bucket in account B. The developer should not need permanent cross-account access. What is the MOST secure way to grant this temporary access?
options: Add the developer's IAM user ARN from account A to the account B S3 bucket policy with s3:GetObject permission, then remove it after the access period
Temporarily add the developer's account A user as an IAM user in account B with S3 read access and delete the user after the work is complete
Create a presigned S3 URL for the specific objects in account B with an expiration time matching the access duration and share the URLs with the developer

Create a cross-account IAM role in account B with S3 read permissions and a trust policy allowing the developer's IAM user in account A to assume it; the developer uses STS AssumeRole to get temporary credentials valid for the duration they need access
explain: Cross-account IAM role assumption with STS is the standard pattern for temporary cross-account access. The role in Account B issues temporary credentials (STS session tokens, default 1 hour, configurable up to 12 hours) that expire automatically — no long-term credentials are created. CloudTrail in Account B records the AssumeRole event for audit. Pre-signed URLs require someone in Account B to generate them per object. Direct bucket policy entries exist until manually removed. Creating IAM users in Account B creates permanent cross-account identity and long-term credentials.
